content.content: 1→# PRD: Basehub CMS Blog for RunAgents 2→ 3→**Author**: Teammate 2 (PRD Writer) 4→**Date**: 2026-02-25 5→**Status**: Draft 6→**Branch**: `development` 7→ 8→--- 9→ 10→## 1. Objective 11→ 12→Add a statically-generated blog to the RunAgents marketing site, powered by Basehub CMS. The blog will live under the `(landing)` route group at `/blog` and `/blog/[slug]`, following the same architectural patterns proven in the ShowCart reference project. Content will be managed entirely within the Basehub dashboard -- no local markdown files, no database tables. 13→ 14→**Goals**: 15→- Provide a public-facing blog for product updates, tutorials, and announcements. 16→- Use Basehub CMS as the single source of truth for blog content. 17→- Achieve full static generation (`force-static`) with `generateStaticParams` for post pages. 18→- Produce proper SEO metadata (Open Graph, title, description) for every page. 19→- Follow RunAgents file conventions (kebab-case files, PascalCase exports, `@/` alias, Biome linting). 20→ 21→--- 22→ 23→## 2. Current State 24→ 25→| Item | Status | 26→|---|---| 27→| `basehub` npm package | Installed (`^9.5.3` in `package.json`) | 28→| Blog routes | None | 29→| Blog components | None | 30→| `BASEHUB_TOKEN` env var | Not configured anywhere | 31→| `package.json` scripts | Standard `next dev` / `next build` -- missing `basehub` codegen prefix | 32→| `.gitignore` entries for Basehub | Missing (`.basehub`, `basehub-types.d.ts`) | 33→| Basehub dashboard schema | Not created | 34→ 35→No blog-related code exists in the codebase. The `basehub` package was added as a dependency but never wired up. 36→ 37→--- 38→ 39→## 3. Basehub Content Schema Required 40→ 41→The following must be created **manually in the Basehub dashboard** before any code will function. This is a one-time setup task. 42→ 43→### 3.1 Top-level block: `Blog` 44→ 45→Create a block named **Blog** at the repository root. 46→ 47→### 3.2 Collection: `Blog > Posts` 48→ 49→Inside the `Blog` block, create a **collection** named **Posts**. Each item in the collection (a "PostsItem") must have these fields: 50→ 51→| Field | Type | Required | Notes | 52→|---|---|---|---| 53→| `_title` | Text (built-in) | Yes | Auto-provided by Basehub for every item | 54→| `_slug` | Slug (built-in) | Yes | Auto-provided, derived from `_title` | 55→| `_id` | ID (built-in) | Yes | Auto-provided | 56→| `date` | Date | Yes | Publication date, used for sorting | 57→| `isvisible` | Boolean | Yes | Toggle to hide/show posts; only `true` posts appear on the site | 58→| `coverImage` | Image | Yes | Fields: `url`, `alt` | 59→| `author` | Block (inline) | Yes | Contains `_title` (author name) and `avatar` (Image with `url`, `alt`) | 60→| `body` | Rich Text | Yes | Supports paragraphs, headings, code blocks, images, links. Exposes `json`, `plainText`, `html`, `markdown`, `readingTime` | 61→ 62→### 3.3 Important Notes 63→ 64→- Field names are **case-sensitive** and must match exactly (e.g., `isvisible`, not `isVisible`). 65→- The `body` rich text field automatically provides `readingTime` and multiple output formats when queried. 66→- The `author` block is inline (nested inside each post), not a separate top-level collection. 67→ 68→--- 69→ 70→## 4. Environment Variables 71→ 72→### 4.1 Add to `.env.example` 73→ 74→Append to the end of `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/.env.example`: 75→ 76→```env 77→# Basehub CMS 78→BASEHUB_TOKEN= 79→# Optional: for draft mode preview (future use) 80→# BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET= 81→``` 82→ 83→### 4.2 Add to `src/env/server.ts` 84→ 85→Add two new entries inside the `server` object of `createEnv`: 86→ 87→```ts 88→// Basehub CMS 89→BASEHUB_TOKEN: z.string().min(1), 90→BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET: z.string().min(1).optional(), 91→``` 92→ 93→Place these after the existing `ENABLE_SIGNUPS` line, before the closing brace of the `server` object. 94→ 95→--- 96→ 97→## 5. Package.json Script Changes 98→ 99→Update the `scripts` section in `package.json`: 100→ 101→| Script | Current | New | 102→|---|---|---| 103→| `dev` | `next dev` | `basehub && next dev` | 104→| `build` | `next build` | `basehub && next build` | 105→| `postinstall` | _(does not exist)_ | `basehub` | 106→ 107→The `basehub` CLI command runs codegen, producing typed helpers and a `.basehub` directory with generated types. The `postinstall` hook ensures types are generated after `pnpm install`. 108→ 109→**Do not change** `start`, `lint`, `format`, or any other existing script. 110→ 111→--- 112→ 113→## 6. File-by-file Implementation Plan 114→ 115→### 6.1 `src/lib/basehub.ts` -- Client Initialization 116→ 117→**Purpose**: Wraps the `basehub()` client constructor with the project's token from `env`. 118→ 119→```ts 120→import { basehub } from "basehub"; 121→import { env } from "@/env/server"; 122→ 123→export const basehubClient = basehub({ token: env.BASEHUB_TOKEN }); 124→``` 125→ 126→- Single export, reused by all server-side query files. 127→- No draft mode logic initially; add later via `BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET`. 128→ 129→--- 130→ 131→### 6.2 `src/lib/basehub.fragment.ts` -- Query Fragments 132→ 133→**Purpose**: Define reusable `fragmentOn` fragments for type-safe Basehub queries. 134→ 135→```ts 136→import { fragmentOn } from "basehub"; 137→ 138→// Lightweight fragment for blog listing cards 139→export const PostMetaFragment = fragmentOn("PostsItem", { 140→ _id: true, 141→ _title: true, 142→ _slug: true, 143→ date: true, 144→ coverImage: { url: true, alt: true }, 145→ author: { 146→ _title: true, 147→ avatar: { url: true, alt: true }, 148→ }, 149→ body: { plainText: true, readingTime: true }, 150→}); 151→ 152→// Full fragment for single post pages 153→export const PostFragment = fragmentOn("PostsItem", { 154→ _id: true, 155→ _title: true, 156→ _slug: true, 157→ date: true, 158→ isvisible: true, 159→ coverImage: { url: true, alt: true }, 160→ author: { 161→ _title: true, 162→ avatar: { url: true, alt: true }, 163→ }, 164→ body: { 165→ json: { content: true }, 166→ plainText: true, 167→ html: true, 168→ markdown: true, 169→ readingTime: true, 170→ }, 171→}); 172→ 173→// Infer TypeScript types from fragments 174→export type PostMeta = fragmentOn.infer; 175→export type Post = fragmentOn.infer; 176→``` 177→ 178→--- 179→ 180→### 6.3 `src/lib/basehub.action.ts` -- Server Actions / Data Fetching 181→ 182→**Purpose**: Server-side functions that query Basehub for blog data. 183→ 184→```ts 185→"use server"; 186→ 187→import { basehubClient } from "@/lib/basehub"; 188→import { PostMetaFragment, PostFragment } from "@/lib/basehub.fragment"; 189→ 190→export async function getAllBlogPosts() { 191→ const { blog } = await basehubClient.query({ 192→ blog: { 193→ posts: { 194→ items: { 195→ ...PostMetaFragment, 196→ isvisible: true, 197→ }, 198→ }, 199→ }, 200→ }); 201→ 202→ return blog.posts.items 203→ .filter((post) => post.isvisible) 204→ .sort((a, b) => new Date(b.date).getTime() - new Date(a.date).getTime()); 205→} 206→ 207→export async function getBlogPost(slug: string) { 208→ const { blog } = await basehubClient.query({ 209→ blog: { 210→ posts: { 211→ items: PostFragment, 212→ }, 213→ }, 214→ }); 215→ 216→ return blog.posts.items.find( 217→ (post) => post._slug === slug && post.isvisible 218→ ) ?? null; 219→} 220→``` 221→ 222→- Filters by `isvisible` on the client side to ensure hidden posts never leak. 223→- `getAllBlogPosts` returns posts sorted newest-first. 224→- `getBlogPost` returns a single post or `null`. 225→ 226→--- 227→ 228→### 6.4 `src/app/(landing)/blog/page.tsx` -- Blog Index Page 229→ 230→**Purpose**: Lists all published blog posts at `/blog`. 231→ 232→Key implementation details: 233→- `export const dynamic = "force-static"` for full static generation. 234→- `export async function generateMetadata()` returns title, description, Open Graph tags. 235→- Calls `getAllBlogPosts()` to fetch posts. 236→- Renders a grid of `PostCard` components. 237→- Shows an empty state message when no posts are published. 238→ 239→Route: `/blog` 240→ 241→--- 242→ 243→### 6.5 `src/app/(landing)/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` -- Single Post Page 244→ 245→**Purpose**: Renders a single blog post at `/blog/[slug]`. 246→ 247→Key implementation details: 248→- `export const dynamic = "force-static"`. 249→- `export async function generateStaticParams()` -- fetches all posts, returns `{ slug }` array. 250→- `export async function generateMetadata({ params })` -- returns per-post SEO metadata (title, description from `plainText` truncated, Open Graph image from `coverImage.url`). 251→- Calls `getBlogPost(slug)`. 252→- If post is `null`, calls `notFound()` from `next/navigation`. 253→- Renders `PostHeader` and `PostBody` components. 254→ 255→Route: `/blog/[slug]` 256→ 257→--- 258→ 259→### 6.6 `src/components/blog/post-card.tsx` -- Post Card for Listings 260→ 261→**Purpose**: Card component used in the blog index grid. 262→ 263→Props: accepts a `PostMeta` object. 264→ 265→Displays: 266→- Cover image via `CoverImage` component. 267→- Post title as a link to `/blog/[slug]`. 268→- Publication date (formatted). 269→- Author name and avatar via `Avatar` component. 270→- Reading time from `body.readingTime`. 271→- Excerpt: first ~160 characters of `body.plainText`. 272→ 273→--- 274→ 275→### 6.7 `src/components/blog/post-body.tsx` -- Rich Text Renderer 276→ 277→**Purpose**: Wraps `RichText` from `basehub/react-rich-text` with custom component overrides. 278→ 279→Props: accepts the `body.json.content` from a full `Post`. 280→ 281→Custom component overrides: 282→- **Image**: renders with `BaseHubImage` from `basehub/next-image` (or a `BodyImage` wrapper). 283→- **Code block**: renders with `CodeBlock` from `basehub/react-code-block`. 284→- **Headings** (`h1`-`h6`): styled with appropriate Tailwind classes. 285→- **Links**: external links get `target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer"`. 286→- **Paragraphs, lists, blockquotes**: styled with Tailwind prose or custom classes. 287→ 288→--- 289→ 290→### 6.8 `src/components/blog/post-header.tsx` -- Post Header 291→ 292→**Purpose**: Renders the top section of a single post page. 293→ 294→Displays: 295→- Post title (`h1`). 296→- Formatted publication date. 297→- Author name and avatar (via `Avatar`). 298→- Cover image (via `CoverImage`), full-width. 299→- Reading time. 300→ 301→--- 302→ 303→### 6.9 `src/components/blog/cover-image.tsx` -- Cover Image Component 304→ 305→**Purpose**: Wrapper around `BaseHubImage` from `basehub/next-image`. 306→ 307→Props: `url: string`, `alt: string`, optional `priority: boolean`. 308→ 309→Uses Next.js image optimization via the Basehub image loader. Sets sensible defaults for `width`, `height`, and `sizes` responsive hints. 310→ 311→--- 312→ 313→### 6.10 `src/components/blog/avatar.tsx` -- Author Avatar 314→ 315→**Purpose**: Displays the author's avatar image and name. 316→ 317→Props: `name: string`, `avatarUrl: string`, `avatarAlt: string`. 318→ 319→Renders a small circular image alongside the author's name. Uses `BaseHubImage` or a standard `` with appropriate sizing. 320→ 321→--- 322→ 323→### 6.11 `src/app/api/draft/route.ts` -- Draft Mode Route (Future Use) 324→ 325→**Purpose**: API route to enable/disable Next.js draft mode for Basehub content previews. 326→ 327→Initial implementation: placeholder that returns 501 Not Implemented. Will be wired up later when `BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET` is configured and draft preview workflow is needed. 328→ 329→This is **low priority** and can be deferred to a follow-up PR. 330→ 331→--- 332→ 333→## 7. Routing & SEO Plan 334→ 335→### 7.1 Routes 336→ 337→| Path | Component | Generation | 338→|---|---|---| 339→| `/blog` | `src/app/(landing)/blog/page.tsx` | `force-static` | 340→| `/blog/[slug]` | `src/app/(landing)/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` | `force-static` + `generateStaticParams` | 341→ 342→### 7.2 SEO: `generateMetadata` 343→ 344→**Blog index** (`/blog`): 345→```ts 346→{ 347→ title: "Blog | RunAgents", 348→ description: "Latest updates, tutorials, and insights from the RunAgents team.", 349→ openGraph: { 350→ title: "Blog | RunAgents", 351→ description: "...", 352→ url: "/blog", 353→ }, 354→} 355→``` 356→ 357→**Single post** (`/blog/[slug]`): 358→```ts 359→{ 360→ title: `${post._title} | RunAgents Blog`, 361→ description: post.body.plainText.slice(0, 160) + "...", 362→ openGraph: { 363→ title: post._title, 364→ description: "...", 365→ images: [{ url: post.coverImage.url }], 366→ type: "article", 367→ publishedTime: post.date, 368→ }, 369→} 370→``` 371→ 372→### 7.3 Sitemap 373→ 374→Consider adding blog URLs to the existing sitemap (if one exists) or creating `src/app/sitemap.ts` that includes all published blog post URLs. This is optional for the initial PR but recommended. 375→ 376→--- 377→ 378→## 8. .gitignore Updates 379→ 380→Add the following two entries to `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/.gitignore`: 381→ 382→``` 383→# Basehub codegen output 384→.basehub 385→basehub-types.d.ts 386→``` 387→ 388→These are generated files produced by the `basehub` CLI and must not be committed. 389→ 390→--- 391→ 392→## 9. Build & Test Plan 393→ 394→### 9.1 Local Development 395→ 396→1. Obtain a Basehub API token from the Basehub dashboard. 397→2. Set `BASEHUB_TOKEN` in `.env`. 398→3. Create the Basehub schema (Section 3) and add at least one test post with `isvisible: true`. 399→4. Run `pnpm dev` -- this now runs `basehub && next dev`. 400→5. Verify `/blog` renders the post listing. 401→6. Verify `/blog/[slug]` renders the full post. 402→7. Verify hidden posts (`isvisible: false`) do not appear. 403→ 404→### 9.2 Build Verification 405→ 406→1. Run `pnpm build`. 407→2. Confirm no TypeScript errors related to Basehub types. 408→3. Confirm static pages are generated for `/blog` and each `/blog/[slug]`. 409→4. Run `pnpm start` and verify pages load correctly. 410→ 411→### 9.3 Edge Cases to Test 412→ 413→- Blog index with zero published posts (empty state). 414→- Navigating to `/blog/nonexistent-slug` returns 404 via `notFound()`. 415→- Post with no cover image (if schema allows optional -- currently required, but handle gracefully). 416→- Post body with code blocks, images, and rich formatting. 417→- Long post titles and descriptions truncate properly in metadata. 418→ 419→### 9.4 Biome Linting 420→ 421→Run `pnpm lint` and `pnpm format` to ensure all new files pass Biome checks. 422→ 423→--- 424→ 425→## 10. Acceptance Criteria 426→ 427→- [ ] `BASEHUB_TOKEN` is documented in `.env.example` and validated in `src/env/server.ts`. 428→- [ ] `package.json` scripts prefixed with `basehub &&` for `dev` and `build`; `postinstall: "basehub"` added. 429→- [ ] `.gitignore` includes `.basehub` and `basehub-types.d.ts`. 430→- [ ] `src/lib/basehub.ts` initializes the Basehub client with the env token. 431→- [ ] `src/lib/basehub.fragment.ts` exports `PostMetaFragment`, `PostFragment`, and their inferred types. 432→- [ ] `src/lib/basehub.action.ts` exports `getAllBlogPosts()` and `getBlogPost(slug)`. 433→- [ ] `/blog` route renders a grid of published posts, sorted by date descending. 434→- [ ] `/blog/[slug]` route renders a full post with rich text, cover image, author info, and reading time. 435→- [ ] `/blog/[slug]` returns 404 for invalid slugs and hidden posts. 436→- [ ] `generateMetadata` produces correct title, description, and Open Graph tags for both routes. 437→- [ ] `generateStaticParams` pre-renders all published post slugs at build time. 438→- [ ] All components use kebab-case filenames, PascalCase exports, and the `@/` import alias. 439→- [ ] All new files pass `pnpm lint` and `pnpm format` without errors. 440→- [ ] `pnpm build` succeeds with no type errors. 441→- [ ] Rich text body renders code blocks, images, headings, links, and lists correctly. 442→ 443→--- 444→ 445→## 11. Manual Steps for Developer 446→ 447→These steps cannot be automated and must be performed by the developer before or during implementation. 448→ 449→### Before Coding 450→ 451→1. **Create a Basehub account/repository** (if not already done) at [basehub.com](https://basehub.com). 452→2. **Create the content schema** in the Basehub dashboard exactly as described in Section 3. 453→3. **Generate an API token** from the Basehub dashboard (Settings > API Tokens). Copy it. 454→4. **Add `BASEHUB_TOKEN`** to your local `.env` file. 455→5. **Create at least one test post** in the Basehub dashboard with `isvisible: true`, a cover image, an author with avatar, and some rich text body content. 456→ 457→### After Coding 458→ 459→6. **Run `pnpm install`** to trigger the `postinstall` basehub codegen. 460→7. **Run `pnpm dev`** and verify `/blog` and `/blog/[slug]` work. 461→8. **Add `BASEHUB_TOKEN`** to Vercel environment variables (or your deployment platform) for production and preview environments. 462→9. **Set up Basehub webhook** (optional) to trigger Vercel redeployment when content changes. This ensures the static blog stays up to date. 463→ 464→### Deployment Checklist 465→ 466→10. Confirm `BASEHUB_TOKEN` is set in the deployment environment. 467→11. Confirm `pnpm build` succeeds in CI/CD. 468→12. Confirm `/blog` is accessible on the deployed URL. 469→ 470→--- 471→ 472→## Appendix: File Tree Summary 473→ 474→``` 475→src/ 476→ lib/ 477→ basehub.ts # Client initialization 478→ basehub.action.ts # Server data fetching functions 479→ basehub.fragment.ts # Query fragments and types 480→ app/ 481→ (landing)/ 482→ blog/ 483→ page.tsx # Blog index 484→ [slug]/ 485→ page.tsx # Single post 486→ api/ 487→ draft/ 488→ route.ts # Draft mode (future) 489→ components/ 490→ blog/ 491→ post-card.tsx # Card for blog listing 492→ post-body.tsx # Rich text renderer 493→ post-header.tsx # Post page header 494→ cover-image.tsx # Cover image wrapper 495→ avatar.tsx # Author avatar 496→``` 497→ 498→--- 499→ 500→## Appendix: Dependency on ShowCart Reference 501→ 502→This PRD is modeled after the Basehub integration in the ShowCart project (`/private/tmp/showcart`). Key patterns adopted: 503→ 504→- `fragmentOn` for typed query fragments. 505→- `RichText` from `basehub/react-rich-text` with custom overrides. 506→- `BaseHubImage` from `basehub/next-image`. 507→- `CodeBlock` from `basehub/react-code-block`. 508→- Static generation with `force-static` and `generateStaticParams`. 509→- No Pump component, no draft mode in initial implementation. 510→

file.content: # PRD: Basehub CMS Blog for RunAgents **Author**: Teammate 2 (PRD Writer) **Date**: 2026-02-25 **Status**: Draft **Branch**: `development` --- ## 1. Objective Add a statically-generated blog to the RunAgents marketing site, powered by Basehub CMS. The blog will live under the `(landing)` route group at `/blog` and `/blog/[slug]`, following the same architectural patterns proven in the ShowCart reference project. Content will be managed entirely within the Basehub dashboard -- no local markdown files, no database tables. **Goals**: - Provide a public-facing blog for product updates, tutorials, and announcements. - Use Basehub CMS as the single source of truth for blog content. - Achieve full static generation (`force-static`) with `generateStaticParams` for post pages. - Produce proper SEO metadata (Open Graph, title, description) for every page. - Follow RunAgents file conventions (kebab-case files, PascalCase exports, `@/` alias, Biome linting). --- ## 2. Current State | Item | Status | |---|---| | `basehub` npm package | Installed (`^9.5.3` in `package.json`) | | Blog routes | None | | Blog components | None | | `BASEHUB_TOKEN` env var | Not configured anywhere | | `package.json` scripts | Standard `next dev` / `next build` -- missing `basehub` codegen prefix | | `.gitignore` entries for Basehub | Missing (`.basehub`, `basehub-types.d.ts`) | | Basehub dashboard schema | Not created | No blog-related code exists in the codebase. The `basehub` package was added as a dependency but never wired up. --- ## 3. Basehub Content Schema Required The following must be created **manually in the Basehub dashboard** before any code will function. This is a one-time setup task. ### 3.1 Top-level block: `Blog` Create a block named **Blog** at the repository root. ### 3.2 Collection: `Blog > Posts` Inside the `Blog` block, create a **collection** named **Posts**. Each item in the collection (a "PostsItem") must have these fields: | Field | Type | Required | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | `_title` | Text (built-in) | Yes | Auto-provided by Basehub for every item | | `_slug` | Slug (built-in) | Yes | Auto-provided, derived from `_title` | | `_id` | ID (built-in) | Yes | Auto-provided | | `date` | Date | Yes | Publication date, used for sorting | | `isvisible` | Boolean | Yes | Toggle to hide/show posts; only `true` posts appear on the site | | `coverImage` | Image | Yes | Fields: `url`, `alt` | | `author` | Block (inline) | Yes | Contains `_title` (author name) and `avatar` (Image with `url`, `alt`) | | `body` | Rich Text | Yes | Supports paragraphs, headings, code blocks, images, links. Exposes `json`, `plainText`, `html`, `markdown`, `readingTime` | ### 3.3 Important Notes - Field names are **case-sensitive** and must match exactly (e.g., `isvisible`, not `isVisible`). - The `body` rich text field automatically provides `readingTime` and multiple output formats when queried. - The `author` block is inline (nested inside each post), not a separate top-level collection. --- ## 4. Environment Variables ### 4.1 Add to `.env.example` Append to the end of `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/.env.example`: ```env # Basehub CMS BASEHUB_TOKEN= # Optional: for draft mode preview (future use) # BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET= ``` ### 4.2 Add to `src/env/server.ts` Add two new entries inside the `server` object of `createEnv`: ```ts // Basehub CMS BASEHUB_TOKEN: z.string().min(1), BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET: z.string().min(1).optional(), ``` Place these after the existing `ENABLE_SIGNUPS` line, before the closing brace of the `server` object. --- ## 5. Package.json Script Changes Update the `scripts` section in `package.json`: | Script | Current | New | |---|---|---| | `dev` | `next dev` | `basehub && next dev` | | `build` | `next build` | `basehub && next build` | | `postinstall` | _(does not exist)_ | `basehub` | The `basehub` CLI command runs codegen, producing typed helpers and a `.basehub` directory with generated types. The `postinstall` hook ensures types are generated after `pnpm install`. **Do not change** `start`, `lint`, `format`, or any other existing script. --- ## 6. File-by-file Implementation Plan ### 6.1 `src/lib/basehub.ts` -- Client Initialization **Purpose**: Wraps the `basehub()` client constructor with the project's token from `env`. ```ts import { basehub } from "basehub"; import { env } from "@/env/server"; export const basehubClient = basehub({ token: env.BASEHUB_TOKEN }); ``` - Single export, reused by all server-side query files. - No draft mode logic initially; add later via `BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET`. --- ### 6.2 `src/lib/basehub.fragment.ts` -- Query Fragments **Purpose**: Define reusable `fragmentOn` fragments for type-safe Basehub queries. ```ts import { fragmentOn } from "basehub"; // Lightweight fragment for blog listing cards export const PostMetaFragment = fragmentOn("PostsItem", { _id: true, _title: true, _slug: true, date: true, coverImage: { url: true, alt: true }, author: { _title: true, avatar: { url: true, alt: true }, }, body: { plainText: true, readingTime: true }, }); // Full fragment for single post pages export const PostFragment = fragmentOn("PostsItem", { _id: true, _title: true, _slug: true, date: true, isvisible: true, coverImage: { url: true, alt: true }, author: { _title: true, avatar: { url: true, alt: true }, }, body: { json: { content: true }, plainText: true, html: true, markdown: true, readingTime: true, }, }); // Infer TypeScript types from fragments export type PostMeta = fragmentOn.infer; export type Post = fragmentOn.infer; ``` --- ### 6.3 `src/lib/basehub.action.ts` -- Server Actions / Data Fetching **Purpose**: Server-side functions that query Basehub for blog data. ```ts "use server"; import { basehubClient } from "@/lib/basehub"; import { PostMetaFragment, PostFragment } from "@/lib/basehub.fragment"; export async function getAllBlogPosts() { const { blog } = await basehubClient.query({ blog: { posts: { items: { ...PostMetaFragment, isvisible: true, }, }, }, }); return blog.posts.items .filter((post) => post.isvisible) .sort((a, b) => new Date(b.date).getTime() - new Date(a.date).getTime()); } export async function getBlogPost(slug: string) { const { blog } = await basehubClient.query({ blog: { posts: { items: PostFragment, }, }, }); return blog.posts.items.find( (post) => post._slug === slug && post.isvisible ) ?? null; } ``` - Filters by `isvisible` on the client side to ensure hidden posts never leak. - `getAllBlogPosts` returns posts sorted newest-first. - `getBlogPost` returns a single post or `null`. --- ### 6.4 `src/app/(landing)/blog/page.tsx` -- Blog Index Page **Purpose**: Lists all published blog posts at `/blog`. Key implementation details: - `export const dynamic = "force-static"` for full static generation. - `export async function generateMetadata()` returns title, description, Open Graph tags. - Calls `getAllBlogPosts()` to fetch posts. - Renders a grid of `PostCard` components. - Shows an empty state message when no posts are published. Route: `/blog` --- ### 6.5 `src/app/(landing)/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` -- Single Post Page **Purpose**: Renders a single blog post at `/blog/[slug]`. Key implementation details: - `export const dynamic = "force-static"`. - `export async function generateStaticParams()` -- fetches all posts, returns `{ slug }` array. - `export async function generateMetadata({ params })` -- returns per-post SEO metadata (title, description from `plainText` truncated, Open Graph image from `coverImage.url`). - Calls `getBlogPost(slug)`. - If post is `null`, calls `notFound()` from `next/navigation`. - Renders `PostHeader` and `PostBody` components. Route: `/blog/[slug]` --- ### 6.6 `src/components/blog/post-card.tsx` -- Post Card for Listings **Purpose**: Card component used in the blog index grid. Props: accepts a `PostMeta` object. Displays: - Cover image via `CoverImage` component. - Post title as a link to `/blog/[slug]`. - Publication date (formatted). - Author name and avatar via `Avatar` component. - Reading time from `body.readingTime`. - Excerpt: first ~160 characters of `body.plainText`. --- ### 6.7 `src/components/blog/post-body.tsx` -- Rich Text Renderer **Purpose**: Wraps `RichText` from `basehub/react-rich-text` with custom component overrides. Props: accepts the `body.json.content` from a full `Post`. Custom component overrides: - **Image**: renders with `BaseHubImage` from `basehub/next-image` (or a `BodyImage` wrapper). - **Code block**: renders with `CodeBlock` from `basehub/react-code-block`. - **Headings** (`h1`-`h6`): styled with appropriate Tailwind classes. - **Links**: external links get `target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer"`. - **Paragraphs, lists, blockquotes**: styled with Tailwind prose or custom classes. --- ### 6.8 `src/components/blog/post-header.tsx` -- Post Header **Purpose**: Renders the top section of a single post page. Displays: - Post title (`h1`). - Formatted publication date. - Author name and avatar (via `Avatar`). - Cover image (via `CoverImage`), full-width. - Reading time. --- ### 6.9 `src/components/blog/cover-image.tsx` -- Cover Image Component **Purpose**: Wrapper around `BaseHubImage` from `basehub/next-image`. Props: `url: string`, `alt: string`, optional `priority: boolean`. Uses Next.js image optimization via the Basehub image loader. Sets sensible defaults for `width`, `height`, and `sizes` responsive hints. --- ### 6.10 `src/components/blog/avatar.tsx` -- Author Avatar **Purpose**: Displays the author's avatar image and name. Props: `name: string`, `avatarUrl: string`, `avatarAlt: string`. Renders a small circular image alongside the author's name. Uses `BaseHubImage` or a standard `` with appropriate sizing. --- ### 6.11 `src/app/api/draft/route.ts` -- Draft Mode Route (Future Use) **Purpose**: API route to enable/disable Next.js draft mode for Basehub content previews. Initial implementation: placeholder that returns 501 Not Implemented. Will be wired up later when `BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET` is configured and draft preview workflow is needed. This is **low priority** and can be deferred to a follow-up PR. --- ## 7. Routing & SEO Plan ### 7.1 Routes | Path | Component | Generation | |---|---|---| | `/blog` | `src/app/(landing)/blog/page.tsx` | `force-static` | | `/blog/[slug]` | `src/app/(landing)/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` | `force-static` + `generateStaticParams` | ### 7.2 SEO: `generateMetadata` **Blog index** (`/blog`): ```ts { title: "Blog | RunAgents", description: "Latest updates, tutorials, and insights from the RunAgents team.", openGraph: { title: "Blog | RunAgents", description: "...", url: "/blog", }, } ``` **Single post** (`/blog/[slug]`): ```ts { title: `${post._title} | RunAgents Blog`, description: post.body.plainText.slice(0, 160) + "...", openGraph: { title: post._title, description: "...", images: [{ url: post.coverImage.url }], type: "article", publishedTime: post.date, }, } ``` ### 7.3 Sitemap Consider adding blog URLs to the existing sitemap (if one exists) or creating `src/app/sitemap.ts` that includes all published blog post URLs. This is optional for the initial PR but recommended. --- ## 8. .gitignore Updates Add the following two entries to `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/.gitignore`: ``` # Basehub codegen output .basehub basehub-types.d.ts ``` These are generated files produced by the `basehub` CLI and must not be committed. --- ## 9. Build & Test Plan ### 9.1 Local Development 1. Obtain a Basehub API token from the Basehub dashboard. 2. Set `BASEHUB_TOKEN` in `.env`. 3. Create the Basehub schema (Section 3) and add at least one test post with `isvisible: true`. 4. Run `pnpm dev` -- this now runs `basehub && next dev`. 5. Verify `/blog` renders the post listing. 6. Verify `/blog/[slug]` renders the full post. 7. Verify hidden posts (`isvisible: false`) do not appear. ### 9.2 Build Verification 1. Run `pnpm build`. 2. Confirm no TypeScript errors related to Basehub types. 3. Confirm static pages are generated for `/blog` and each `/blog/[slug]`. 4. Run `pnpm start` and verify pages load correctly. ### 9.3 Edge Cases to Test - Blog index with zero published posts (empty state). - Navigating to `/blog/nonexistent-slug` returns 404 via `notFound()`. - Post with no cover image (if schema allows optional -- currently required, but handle gracefully). - Post body with code blocks, images, and rich formatting. - Long post titles and descriptions truncate properly in metadata. ### 9.4 Biome Linting Run `pnpm lint` and `pnpm format` to ensure all new files pass Biome checks. --- ## 10. Acceptance Criteria - [ ] `BASEHUB_TOKEN` is documented in `.env.example` and validated in `src/env/server.ts`. - [ ] `package.json` scripts prefixed with `basehub &&` for `dev` and `build`; `postinstall: "basehub"` added. - [ ] `.gitignore` includes `.basehub` and `basehub-types.d.ts`. - [ ] `src/lib/basehub.ts` initializes the Basehub client with the env token. - [ ] `src/lib/basehub.fragment.ts` exports `PostMetaFragment`, `PostFragment`, and their inferred types. - [ ] `src/lib/basehub.action.ts` exports `getAllBlogPosts()` and `getBlogPost(slug)`. - [ ] `/blog` route renders a grid of published posts, sorted by date descending. - [ ] `/blog/[slug]` route renders a full post with rich text, cover image, author info, and reading time. - [ ] `/blog/[slug]` returns 404 for invalid slugs and hidden posts. - [ ] `generateMetadata` produces correct title, description, and Open Graph tags for both routes. - [ ] `generateStaticParams` pre-renders all published post slugs at build time. - [ ] All components use kebab-case filenames, PascalCase exports, and the `@/` import alias. - [ ] All new files pass `pnpm lint` and `pnpm format` without errors. - [ ] `pnpm build` succeeds with no type errors. - [ ] Rich text body renders code blocks, images, headings, links, and lists correctly. --- ## 11. Manual Steps for Developer These steps cannot be automated and must be performed by the developer before or during implementation. ### Before Coding 1. **Create a Basehub account/repository** (if not already done) at [basehub.com](https://basehub.com). 2. **Create the content schema** in the Basehub dashboard exactly as described in Section 3. 3. **Generate an API token** from the Basehub dashboard (Settings > API Tokens). Copy it. 4. **Add `BASEHUB_TOKEN`** to your local `.env` file. 5. **Create at least one test post** in the Basehub dashboard with `isvisible: true`, a cover image, an author with avatar, and some rich text body content. ### After Coding 6. **Run `pnpm install`** to trigger the `postinstall` basehub codegen. 7. **Run `pnpm dev`** and verify `/blog` and `/blog/[slug]` work. 8. **Add `BASEHUB_TOKEN`** to Vercel environment variables (or your deployment platform) for production and preview environments. 9. **Set up Basehub webhook** (optional) to trigger Vercel redeployment when content changes. This ensures the static blog stays up to date. ### Deployment Checklist 10. Confirm `BASEHUB_TOKEN` is set in the deployment environment. 11. Confirm `pnpm build` succeeds in CI/CD. 12. Confirm `/blog` is accessible on the deployed URL. --- ## Appendix: File Tree Summary ``` src/ lib/ basehub.ts # Client initialization basehub.action.ts # Server data fetching functions basehub.fragment.ts # Query fragments and types app/ (landing)/ blog/ page.tsx # Blog index [slug]/ page.tsx # Single post api/ draft/ route.ts # Draft mode (future) components/ blog/ post-card.tsx # Card for blog listing post-body.tsx # Rich text renderer post-header.tsx # Post page header cover-image.tsx # Cover image wrapper avatar.tsx # Author avatar ``` --- ## Appendix: Dependency on ShowCart Reference This PRD is modeled after the Basehub integration in the ShowCart project (`/private/tmp/showcart`). Key patterns adopted: - `fragmentOn` for typed query fragments. - `RichText` from `basehub/react-rich-text` with custom overrides. - `BaseHubImage` from `basehub/next-image`. - `CodeBlock` from `basehub/react-code-block`. - Static generation with `force-static` and `generateStaticParams`. - No Pump component, no draft mode in initial implementation.

     4→export const env = createEnv({
     5→  server: {
     6→    DATABASE_URL: z.url(),
     7→    BETTER_AUTH_SECRET: z.string().min(1),
     8→    RESEND_API_KEY: z.string().min(1),
     9→    E2B_API_KEY: z.string().min(1),
    10→    OPENROUTER_API_KEY: z.string().min(1),
    11→    NODE_ENV: z
    12→      .enum(["development", "test", "production"])
    13→      .default("development"),
    14→    GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID: z.string().min(1),
    15→    GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET: z.string().min(1),
    16→    EMAIL_DOMAIN: z.string().default("mail.runagents.co"),
    17→    DEV_ACCESS_TOKEN: z.string().min(1).optional(),
    18→    // Dodo Payments configuration
    19→    DODO_PAYMENTS_API_KEY: z.string().min(1),
    20→    // Required: for webhook signature verification
    21→    DODO_PAYMENTS_WEBHOOK_SECRET: z.string().min(1),
    22→    ENABLE_SIGNUPS: z.enum(["true", "false"]).default("true"),
    23→    DODO_PAYMENTS_MODE: z.enum(["live_mode", "test_mode"]),
    24→  },
    25→  experimental__runtimeEnv: process.env,
    26→  skipValidation: !!process.env.SKIP_ENV_VALIDATION,
    27→});

DATABASE_URL=

# Auth
BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=
RESEND_API_KEY=
EMAIL_DOMAIN=mail.runagents.co
GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=

# E2B
E2B_API_KEY=

# AI
OPENROUTER_API_KEY=

# Payments
DODO_PAYMENTS_API_KEY=
DODO_PAYMENTS_WEBHOOK_SECRET=
DODO_PAYMENTS_MODE=test_mode

# Dev gate — set this on your dev Vercel project to restrict access via ?token=<value>
# Leave unset in production and locally (gate is inactive when this var is absent)
DEV_ACCESS_TOKEN=

# Public
NEXT_PUBLIC_SERVER_URL=http://localhost:3000

# Feature Flags
ENABLE_SIGNUPS=true

input.command:
gh pr create \
  --base development \
  --title "feat(blog): Basehub CMS blog pages implementation" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
Implements blog pages powered by Basehub CMS as described in `docs/prd-basehub-blog.md`.
Reference implementation pattern taken from FounderFeedAdmin/ShowCart.

## What's Included
- Blog index page at `/blog` with `force-static` generation
- Dynamic post page at `/blog/[slug]` with `generateStaticParams`
- `PostCard`, `PostHeader`, `PostBody` components
- `RichText` renderer with custom element handlers (headings, code, images, links, lists, blockquotes)
- `BaseHubImage` for CMS-optimized images
- `BASEHUB_TOKEN` env validation via T3 Env + Zod
- Draft mode API route placeholder (`/api/draft`)
- Typed query fragments (`PostMetaFragment`, `PostFragment`) via `fragmentOn`
- Server actions for data fetching (`getAllBlogPosts`, `getBlogPost`, `getAllBlogSlugs`)
- `generateMetadata` with Open Graph tags for both routes
- Updated `dev`/`build` scripts to run `basehub` codegen first
- PRD document at `docs/prd-basehub-blog.md`

## Manual Steps Required Before This Works
1. Create a Basehub account and repository at basehub.com
2. Create the Posts collection schema in Basehub dashboard (see PRD Section 3 for exact fields: `date`, `isvisible`, `coverImage`, `author` with `avatar`, `body` rich text)
3. Copy `BASEHUB_TOKEN` from the Connect tab in Basehub
4. Add to `.env.local`: `BASEHUB_TOKEN=your_token`
5. Run: `pnpm basehub` (to generate type-safe SDK in `.basehub/`)
6. Run: `pnpm dev` (which now runs `basehub && next dev`)

## Testing
- Set env vars → run `pnpm dev` → visit `/blog`
- Verify posts render with cover image, author avatar, date, reading time
- Navigate to `/blog/[slug]` and verify full post with rich text rendering
- Visit `/blog/nonexistent` to verify 404 handling
- Run `pnpm lint` to confirm all files pass Biome

## TypeScript Note
All TS errors in new files are expected — they resolve once `BASEHUB_TOKEN` is set and `pnpm basehub` generates the `.basehub/` typed SDK. Without the token, `fragmentOn("PostsItem", ...)` resolves to `never`.

## Checklist
- [ ] BASEHUB_TOKEN added to Vercel environment variables
- [ ] Posts schema created in Basehub dashboard (see PRD Section 3)
- [ ] At least 1 test post created in Basehub with `isvisible: true`
- [ ] `pnpm basehub` run successfully after token is set
- [ ] `postinstall` script verified on Vercel build

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.text:
---

## Team Lead — Final Handoff Summary

All 4 teammates completed successfully. Here's the synthesis:

### What was built:
| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/lib/basehub.ts` | Client initialization with env token |
| `src/lib/basehub.fragment.ts` | `PostMetaFragment` + `PostFragment` typed query fragments |
| `src/lib/basehub.action.ts` | Server actions: `getAllBlogPosts`, `getBlogPost`, `getAllBlogSlugs` |
| `src/app/(landing)/blog/page.tsx` | Blog index at `/blog` with `force-static` |
| `src/app/(landing)/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` | Post page with `generateStaticParams` + `generateMetadata` |
| `src/components/blog/post-card.tsx` | Card component for blog listing |
| `src/components/blog/post-header.tsx` | Title, date, author, cover image header |
| `src/components/blog/post-body.tsx` | RichText renderer with custom handlers |
| `src/components/blog/cover-image.tsx` | BaseHubImage wrapper |
| `src/components/blog/avatar.tsx` | Author avatar component |
| `src/app/api/draft/route.ts` | Draft mode placeholder (501) |
| `docs/prd-basehub-blog.md` | Full PRD document |
| Modified: `package.json`, `.env.example`, `src/env/server.ts`, `.gitignore`, `tsconfig.json`, `basehub.config.ts` |

### PR: https://github.com/FounderFeedAdmin/runagents/pull/19

### SDK codegen status: **Pending token** — `.basehub/` types will generate once `BASEHUB_TOKEN` is set

### Manual steps the developer MUST do before this works:
1. **Create Basehub account + repo** at basehub.com
2. **Create Posts schema** in dashboard: `date` (date), `isvisible` (boolean), `coverImage` (image with url/alt), `author` (block with `_title` + `avatar` image), `body` (rich text)
3. **Add env vars** locally: `BASEHUB_TOKEN=<token from Connect tab>` in `.env.local`; also add to Vercel environment variables
4. **Run `pnpm basehub`** to generate type-safe SDK (resolves all TypeScript errors)
5. **Create at least 1 test post** with `isvisible: true` in Basehub dashboard

### Open questions / follow-ups:
- **Draft preview**: Currently a 501 placeholder. Wire up `Pump` + `draftMode()` + `BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET` when live editing workflow is needed
- **Sitemap**: Blog URLs not yet added to sitemap — consider creating `src/app/sitemap.ts`
- **Navbar "Blog" link**: May want to add a "Blog" entry to the landing page navbar (`NAV_LINKS` in `navbar.tsx`)
- **ISR revalidation**: Currently `force-static` with no revalidation. Consider adding Basehub webhook to trigger Vercel redeployment on content changes

content.content:
1→# Project Instructions: runagents
     2→
     3→## Tech Stack
     4→- **Framework**: Next.js (App Router)
     5→- **Package Manager**: `pnpm`
     6→- **Environment**: T3 Env + Zod
     7→- **Database**: Neon + Drizzle ORM
     8→- **Auth**: Better Auth + Better Auth UI
     9→- **Styling**: Tailwind CSS + Shadcn UI
    10→- **State**: Zustand (UI only), React Query (server data)
    11→- **Lint/Format**: Biome
    12→
    13→## Environment
    14→- Secrets in `.env` (gitignored), template in `.env.example`.
    15→
    16→## File Naming
    17→- **Files**: `kebab-case.tsx` — `agent-card.tsx`, `agent.actions.ts`, `agent.repo.ts`
    18→- **Exports**: `PascalCase` — `export function AgentCard() {}`
    19→
    20→## Directory Layout
    21→
    22→```
    23→src/
    24→├── app/
    25→│   ├── agents/
    26→│   │   ├── page.tsx
    27→│   │   ├── [id]/page.tsx
    28→│   │   ├── _components/               # Colocated, private (not a route)
    29→│   │   ├── _actions/                  # Server actions for this route
    30→│   │   └── _hooks/                    # React Query hooks for this route
    31→│   ├── sign-in/page.tsx
    32→│   ├── sign-up/page.tsx
    33→│   ├── api/auth/[...better-auth]/route.ts
    34→│   ├── layout.tsx
    35→│   ├── page.tsx
    36→│   └── globals.css
    37→├── components/
    38→│   ├── ui/                            # Shadcn primitives (CLI-managed, don't edit)
    39→│   └── *.tsx                          # Shared composed components (flat)
    40→├── hooks/                             # Shared custom hooks
    41→├── lib/                               # External clients & utilities
    42→├── server/
    43→│   ├── action-utils.ts                # authedAction builder, getAuthedSession, parseInput
    44→│   ├── actions/                       # Shared server actions
    45→│   └── db/ (models/, repos/, index.ts)
    46→├── state/                             # Zustand stores
    47→└── env/                               # T3 Env validation
    48→```
    49→
    50→## Placement Rules
    51→
    52→| Used by… | Location |
    53→|---|---|
    54→| 1 page | `_components/`, `_actions/`, `_hooks/` (colocated) |
    55→| 2+ pages | `components/`, `server/actions/`, `hooks/` (promoted) |
    56→| Shadcn primitive | `components/ui/` (CLI-managed) |
    57→
    58→Prefix colocated files with context — `agent-card.tsx` not `card.tsx`.
    59→Keep `components/` flat until 15+ files.
    60→`_components/` only exists inside specific page route folders (e.g. `app/agents/_components/`), never at `app/` root.
    61→
    62→## Server Patterns
    63→
    64→```
    65→Component → React Query hook → Server Action → Repo → DB
    66→```
    67→
    68→| Layer | Location | Does what |
    69→|---|---|---|
    70→| **Repo** | `server/db/repos/` | Pure DB queries. No auth, no validation. |
    71→| **Action** | `_actions/` | Auth + validation + calls repo. |
    72→| **React Query** | `_hooks/` | Client cache, refetching, optimistic UI. Wraps actions. |
    73→
    74→### `authedAction` builder (`server/action-utils.ts`)
    75→
    76→```ts
    77→// Read — auth only
    78→export const getAgents = authedAction(
    79→  async ({ user }) => agentRepo.findAll(user.id),
    80→);
    81→
    82→// Write — auth + Zod validation
    83→export const createAgent = authedAction(createAgentSchema,
    84→  async ({ user }, input) => agentRepo.create({ ...input, userId: user.id }),
    85→);
    86→```
    87→
    88→Also exports `getAuthedSession()` and `parseInput()` for direct use.
    89→
    90→### When to use what
    91→
    92→| Scenario | Pattern |
    93→|---|---|
    94→| Page load, no interactivity | Server Component → repo directly |
    95→| Client needs cache/refetch | React Query → server action → repo |
    96→| Simple form submit | `useTransition` + server action → repo |
    97→| External consumer / webhook | API route → repo |
    98→
    99→## Rules
   100→
   101→1. Only repos import `db`. Actions never query DB directly.
   102→2. Only actions check auth and validate input. Repos are auth-unaware.
   103→3. Server Components can call repos directly, but must check auth via `getAuthedSession()` first.
   104→4. Promote to shared when used by 2+ pages.
   105→5. Default to Server Components. Add `"use client"` only for interactivity. Action files must start with `"use server"`.
   106→6. `@/` maps to `src/`. Always use `@/` imports, not relative paths.
   107→7. Use `pnpm` exclusively.
   108→8. Use variables via `src/env/`, never `process.env` directly.
   109→9. Use `authClient` (client) and `auth` (server) from `@/lib`.
   110→10. Use `npx shadcn@latest add [component]` to install UI components.
   111→11. Keep models modular in `server/db/models/`, types in `server/db/schema.ts`.
   112→12. Use `pnpm db:generate` for migrations, `pnpm db:push` for dev sync. Ask user before running `db:push`.
   113→13. Use `pnpm auth:generate` after modifying auth config.
   114→14. Run `pnpm lint`, `pnpm format`, `pnpm type-check` to verify changes.
   115→

content.content:
> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://code.claude.com/docs/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Extend Claude with skills

> Create, manage, and share skills to extend Claude's capabilities in Claude Code. Includes custom slash commands.

Skills extend what Claude can do. Create a `SKILL.md` file with instructions, and Claude adds it to its toolkit. Claude uses skills when relevant, or you can invoke one directly with `/skill-name`.

<Note>
  For built-in commands like `/help` and `/compact`, see [interactive mode](/en/interactive-mode#built-in-commands).

  **Custom slash commands have been merged into skills.** A file at `.claude/commands/review.md` and a skill at `.claude/skills/review/SKILL.md` both create `/review` and work the same way. Your existing `.claude/commands/` files keep working. Skills add optional features: a directory for supporting files, frontmatter to [control whether you or Claude invokes them](#control-who-invokes-a-skill), and the ability for Claude to load them automatically when relevant.
</Note>

Claude Code skills follow the [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io) open standard, which works across multiple AI tools. Claude Code extends the standard with additional features like [invocation control](#control-who-invokes-a-skill), [subagent execution](#run-skills-in-a-subagent), and [dynamic context injection](#inject-dynamic-context).

## Getting started

### Create your first skill

This example creates a skill that teaches Claude to explain code using visual diagrams and analogies. Since it uses default frontmatter, Claude can load it automatically when you ask how something works, or you can invoke it directly with `/explain-code`.

<Steps>
  <Step title="Create the skill directory">
    Create a directory for the skill in your personal skills folder. Personal skills are available across all your projects.

    ```bash  theme={null}
    mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/explain-code
    ```
  </Step>

  <Step title="Write SKILL.md">
    Every skill needs a `SKILL.md` file with two parts: YAML frontmatter (between `---` markers) that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked. The `name` field becomes the `/slash-command`, and the `description` helps Claude decide when to load it automatically.

    Create `~/.claude/skills/explain-code/SKILL.md`:

    ```yaml  theme={null}
    ---
    name: explain-code
    description: Explains code with visual diagrams and analogies. Use when explaining how code works, teaching about a codebase, or when the user asks "how does this work?"
    ---

    When explaining code, always include:

    1. **Start with an analogy**: Compare the code to something from everyday life
    2. **Draw a diagram**: Use ASCII art to show the flow, structure, or relationships
    3. **Walk through the code**: Explain step-by-step what happens
    4. **Highlight a gotcha**: What's a common mistake or misconception?

    Keep explanations conversational. For complex concepts, use multiple analogies.
    ```
  </Step>

  <Step title="Test the skill">
    You can test it two ways:

    **Let Claude invoke it automatically** by asking something that matches the description:

    ```
    How does this code work?
    ```

    **Or invoke it directly** with the skill name:

    ```
    /explain-code src/auth/login.ts
    ```

    Either way, Claude should include an analogy and ASCII diagram in its explanation.
  </Step>
</Steps>

### Where skills live

Where you store a skill determines who can use it:

| Location   | Path                                                | Applies to                     |
| :--------- | :-------------------------------------------------- | :----------------------------- |
| Enterprise | See [managed settings](/en/settings#settings-files) | All users in your organization |
| Personal   | `~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`            | All your projects              |
| Project    | `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`              | This project only              |
| Plugin     | `<plugin>/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`             | Where plugin is enabled        |

When skills share the same name across levels, higher-priority locations win: enterprise > personal > project. Plugin skills use a `plugin-name:skill-name` namespace, so they cannot conflict with other levels. If you have files in `.claude/commands/`, those work the same way, but if a skill and a command share the same name, the skill takes precedence.

#### Automatic discovery from nested directories

When you work with files in subdirectories, Claude Code automatically discovers skills from nested `.claude/skills/` directories. For example, if you're editing a file in `packages/frontend/`, Claude Code also looks for skills in `packages/frontend/.claude/skills/`. This supports monorepo setups where packages have their own skills.

Each skill is a directory with `SKILL.md` as the entrypoint:

```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md           # Main instructions (required)
├── template.md        # Template for Claude to fill in
├── examples/
│   └── sample.md      # Example output showing expected format
└── scripts/
    └── validate.sh    # Script Claude can execute
```

The `SKILL.md` contains the main instructions and is required. Other files are optional and let you build more powerful skills: templates for Claude to fill in, example outputs showing the expected format, scripts Claude can execute, or detailed reference documentation. Reference these files from your `SKILL.md` so Claude knows what they contain and when to load them. See [Add supporting files](#add-supporting-files) for more details.

<Note>
  Files in `.claude/commands/` still work and support the same [frontmatter](#frontmatter-reference). Skills are recommended since they support additional features like supporting files.
</Note>

#### Skills from additional directories

Skills defined in `.claude/skills/` within directories added via `--add-dir` are loaded automatically and picked up by live change detection, so you can edit them during a session without restarting.

<Note>
  CLAUDE.md files from `--add-dir` directories are not loaded by default. To load them, set `CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD=1`. See [Load memory from additional directories](/en/memory#load-memory-from-additional-directories).
</Note>

## Configure skills

Skills are configured through YAML frontmatter at the top of `SKILL.md` and the markdown content that follows.

### Types of skill content

Skill files can contain any instructions, but thinking about how you want to invoke them helps guide what to include:

**Reference content** adds knowledge Claude applies to your current work. Conventions, patterns, style guides, domain knowledge. This content runs inline so Claude can use it alongside your conversation context.

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: api-conventions
description: API design patterns for this codebase
---

When writing API endpoints:
- Use RESTful naming conventions
- Return consistent error formats
- Include request validation
```

**Task content** gives Claude step-by-step instructions for a specific action, like deployments, commits, or code generation. These are often actions you want to invoke directly with `/skill-name` rather than letting Claude decide when to run them. Add `disable-model-invocation: true` to prevent Claude from triggering it automatically.

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: deploy
description: Deploy the application to production
context: fork
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Deploy the application:
1. Run the test suite
2. Build the application
3. Push to the deployment target
```

Your `SKILL.md` can contain anything, but thinking through how you want the skill invoked (by you, by Claude, or both) and where you want it to run (inline or in a subagent) helps guide what to include. For complex skills, you can also [add supporting files](#add-supporting-files) to keep the main skill focused.

### Frontmatter reference

Beyond the markdown content, you can configure skill behavior using YAML frontmatter fields between `---` markers at the top of your `SKILL.md` file:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: my-skill
description: What this skill does
disable-model-invocation: true
allowed-tools: Read, Grep
---

Your skill instructions here...
```

All fields are optional. Only `description` is recommended so Claude knows when to use the skill.

| Field                      | Required    | Description                                                                                                                                           |
| :------------------------- | :---------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `name`                     | No          | Display name for the skill. If omitted, uses the directory name. Lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only (max 64 characters).                    |
| `description`              | Recommended | What the skill does and when to use it. Claude uses this to decide when to apply the skill. If omitted, uses the first paragraph of markdown content. |
| `argument-hint`            | No          | Hint shown during autocomplete to indicate expected arguments. Example: `[issue-number]` or `[filename] [format]`.                                    |
| `disable-model-invocation` | No          | Set to `true` to prevent Claude from automatically loading this skill. Use for workflows you want to trigger manually with `/name`. Default: `false`. |
| `user-invocable`           | No          | Set to `false` to hide from the `/` menu. Use for background knowledge users shouldn't invoke directly. Default: `true`.                              |
| `allowed-tools`            | No          | Tools Claude can use without asking permission when this skill is active.                                                                             |
| `model`                    | No          | Model to use when this skill is active.                                                                                                               |
| `context`                  | No          | Set to `fork` to run in a forked subagent context.                                                                                                    |
| `agent`                    | No          | Which subagent type to use when `context: fork` is set.                                                                                               |
| `hooks`                    | No          | Hooks scoped to this skill's lifecycle. See [Hooks in skills and agents](/en/hooks#hooks-in-skills-and-agents) for configuration format.              |

#### Available string substitutions

Skills support string substitution for dynamic values in the skill content:

| Variable               | Description                                                                                                                                  |
| :--------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `$ARGUMENTS`           | All arguments passed when invoking the skill. If `$ARGUMENTS` is not present in the content, arguments are appended as `ARGUMENTS: <value>`. |
| `$ARGUMENTS[N]`        | Access a specific argument by 0-based index, such as `$ARGUMENTS[0]` for the first argument.                                                 |
| `$N`                   | Shorthand for `$ARGUMENTS[N]`, such as `$0` for the first argument or `$1` for the second.                                                   |
| `${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}` | The current session ID. Useful for logging, creating session-specific files, or correlating skill output with sessions.                      |

**Example using substitutions:**

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: session-logger
description: Log activity for this session
---

Log the following to logs/${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}.log:

$ARGUMENTS
```

### Add supporting files

Skills can include multiple files in their directory. This keeps `SKILL.md` focused on the essentials while letting Claude access detailed reference material only when needed. Large reference docs, API specifications, or example collections don't need to load into context every time the skill runs.

```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md (required - overview and navigation)
├── reference.md (detailed API docs - loaded when needed)
├── examples.md (usage examples - loaded when needed)
└── scripts/
    └── helper.py (utility script - executed, not loaded)
```

Reference supporting files from `SKILL.md` so Claude knows what each file contains and when to load it:

```markdown  theme={null}
## Additional resources

- For complete API details, see [reference.md](reference.md)
- For usage examples, see [examples.md](examples.md)
```

<Tip>Keep `SKILL.md` under 500 lines. Move detailed reference material to separate files.</Tip>

### Control who invokes a skill

By default, both you and Claude can invoke any skill. You can type `/skill-name` to invoke it directly, and Claude can load it automatically when relevant to your conversation. Two frontmatter fields let you restrict this:

* **`disable-model-invocation: true`**: Only you can invoke the skill. Use this for workflows with side effects or that you want to control timing, like `/commit`, `/deploy`, or `/send-slack-message`. You don't want Claude deciding to deploy because your code looks ready.

* **`user-invocable: false`**: Only Claude can invoke the skill. Use this for background knowledge that isn't actionable as a command. A `legacy-system-context` skill explains how an old system works. Claude should know this when relevant, but `/legacy-system-context` isn't a meaningful action for users to take.

This example creates a deploy skill that only you can trigger. The `disable-model-invocation: true` field prevents Claude from running it automatically:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: deploy
description: Deploy the application to production
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Deploy $ARGUMENTS to production:

1. Run the test suite
2. Build the application
3. Push to the deployment target
4. Verify the deployment succeeded
```

Here's how the two fields affect invocation and context loading:

| Frontmatter                      | You can invoke | Claude can invoke | When loaded into context                                     |
| :------------------------------- | :------------- | :---------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------- |
| (default)                        | Yes            | Yes               | Description always in context, full skill loads when invoked |
| `disable-model-invocation: true` | Yes            | No                | Description not in context, full skill loads when you invoke |
| `user-invocable: false`          | No             | Yes               | Description always in context, full skill loads when invoked |

<Note>
  In a regular session, skill descriptions are loaded into context so Claude knows what's available, but full skill content only loads when invoked. [Subagents with preloaded skills](/en/sub-agents#preload-skills-into-subagents) work differently: the full skill content is injected at startup.
</Note>

### Restrict tool access

Use the `allowed-tools` field to limit which tools Claude can use when a skill is active. This skill creates a read-only mode where Claude can explore files but not modify them:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: safe-reader
description: Read files without making changes
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob
---
```

### Pass arguments to skills

Both you and Claude can pass arguments when invoking a skill. Arguments are available via the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder.

This skill fixes a GitHub issue by number. The `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder gets replaced with whatever follows the skill name:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: fix-issue
description: Fix a GitHub issue
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Fix GitHub issue $ARGUMENTS following our coding standards.

1. Read the issue description
2. Understand the requirements
3. Implement the fix
4. Write tests
5. Create a commit
```

When you run `/fix-issue 123`, Claude receives "Fix GitHub issue 123 following our coding standards..."

If you invoke a skill with arguments but the skill doesn't include `$ARGUMENTS`, Claude Code appends `ARGUMENTS: <your input>` to the end of the skill content so Claude still sees what you typed.

To access individual arguments by position, use `$ARGUMENTS[N]` or the shorter `$N`:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: migrate-component
description: Migrate a component from one framework to another
---

Migrate the $ARGUMENTS[0] component from $ARGUMENTS[1] to $ARGUMENTS[2].
Preserve all existing behavior and tests.
```

Running `/migrate-component SearchBar React Vue` replaces `$ARGUMENTS[0]` with `SearchBar`, `$ARGUMENTS[1]` with `React`, and `$ARGUMENTS[2]` with `Vue`. The same skill using the `$N` shorthand:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: migrate-component
description: Migrate a component from one framework to another
---

Migrate the $0 component from $1 to $2.
Preserve all existing behavior and tests.
```

## Advanced patterns

### Inject dynamic context

The `!`command\`\` syntax runs shell commands before the skill content is sent to Claude. The command output replaces the placeholder, so Claude receives actual data, not the command itself.

This skill summarizes a pull request by fetching live PR data with the GitHub CLI. The `!`gh pr diff\`\` and other commands run first, and their output gets inserted into the prompt:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: pr-summary
description: Summarize changes in a pull request
context: fork
agent: Explore
allowed-tools: Bash(gh *)
---

## Pull request context
- PR diff: !`gh pr diff`
- PR comments: !`gh pr view --comments`
- Changed files: !`gh pr diff --name-only`

## Your task
Summarize this pull request...
```

When this skill runs:

1. Each `!`command\`\` executes immediately (before Claude sees anything)
2. The output replaces the placeholder in the skill content
3. Claude receives the fully-rendered prompt with actual PR data

This is preprocessing, not something Claude executes. Claude only sees the final result.

<Tip>
  To enable [extended thinking](/en/common-workflows#use-extended-thinking-thinking-mode) in a skill, include the word "ultrathink" anywhere in your skill content.
</Tip>

### Run skills in a subagent

Add `context: fork` to your frontmatter when you want a skill to run in isolation. The skill content becomes the prompt that drives the subagent. It won't have access to your conversation history.

<Warning>
  `context: fork` only makes sense for skills with explicit instructions. If your skill contains guidelines like "use these API conventions" without a task, the subagent receives the guidelines but no actionable prompt, and returns without meaningful output.
</Warning>

Skills and [subagents](/en/sub-agents) work together in two directions:

| Approach                     | System prompt                             | Task                        | Also loads                   |
| :--------------------------- | :---------------------------------------- | :-------------------------- | :--------------------------- |
| Skill with `context: fork`   | From agent type (`Explore`, `Plan`, etc.) | SKILL.md content            | CLAUDE.md                    |
| Subagent with `skills` field | Subagent's markdown body                  | Claude's delegation message | Preloaded skills + CLAUDE.md |

With `context: fork`, you write the task in your skill and pick an agent type to execute it. For the inverse (defining a custom subagent that uses skills as reference material), see [Subagents](/en/sub-agents#preload-skills-into-subagents).

#### Example: Research skill using Explore agent

This skill runs research in a forked Explore agent. The skill content becomes the task, and the agent provides read-only tools optimized for codebase exploration:

```yaml  theme={null}
---
name: deep-research
description: Research a topic thoroughly
context: fork
agent: Explore
---

Research $ARGUMENTS thoroughly:

1. Find relevant files using Glob and Grep
2. Read and analyze the code
3. Summarize findings with specific file references
```

When this skill runs:

1. A new isolated context is created
2. The subagent receives the skill content as its prompt ("Research \$ARGUMENTS thoroughly...")
3. The `agent` field determines the execution environment (model, tools, and permissions)
4. Results are summarized and returned to your main conversation

The `agent` field specifies which subagent configuration to use. Options include built-in agents (`Explore`, `Plan`, `general-purpose`) or any custom subagent from `.claude/agents/`. If omitted, uses `general-purpose`.

### Restrict Claude's skill access

By default, Claude can invoke any skill that doesn't have `disable-model-invocation: true` set. Skills that define `allowed-tools` grant Claude access to those tools without per-use approval when the skill is active. Your [permission settings](/en/permissions) still govern baseline approval behavior for all other tools. Built-in commands like `/compact` and `/init` are not available through the Skill tool.

Three ways to control which skills Claude can invoke:

**Disable all skills** by denying the Skill tool in `/permissions`:

```
# Add to deny rules:
Skill
```

**Allow or deny specific skills** using [permission rules](/en/permissions):

```
# Allow only specific skills
Skill(commit)
Skill(review-pr *)

# Deny specific skills
Skill(deploy *)
```

Permission syntax: `Skill(name)` for exact match, `Skill(name *)` for prefix match with any arguments.

**Hide individual skills** by adding `disable-model-invocation: true` to their frontmatter. This removes the skill from Claude's context entirely.

<Note>
  The `user-invocable` field only controls menu visibility, not Skill tool access. Use `disable-model-invocation: true` to block programmatic invocation.
</Note>

## Share skills

Skills can be distributed at different scopes depending on your audience:

* **Project skills**: Commit `.claude/skills/` to version control
* **Plugins**: Create a `skills/` directory in your [plugin](/en/plugins)
* **Managed**: Deploy organization-wide through [managed settings](/en/settings#settings-files)

### Generate visual output

Skills can bundle and run scripts in any language, giving Claude capabilities beyond what's possible in a single prompt. One powerful pattern is generating visual output: interactive HTML files that open in your browser for exploring data, debugging, or creating reports.

This example creates a codebase explorer: an interactive tree view where you can expand and collapse directories, see file sizes at a glance, and identify file types by color.

Create the Skill directory:

```bash  theme={null}
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/codebase-visualizer/scripts
```

Create `~/.claude/skills/codebase-visualizer/SKILL.md`. The description tells Claude when to activate this Skill, and the instructions tell Claude to run the bundled script:

````yaml  theme={null}
---
name: codebase-visualizer
description: Generate an interactive collapsible tree visualization of your codebase. Use when exploring a new repo, understanding project structure, or identifying large files.
allowed-tools: Bash(python *)
---

# Codebase Visualizer

Generate an interactive HTML tree view that shows your project's file structure with collapsible directories.

## Usage

Run the visualization script from your project root:

```bash
python ~/.claude/skills/codebase-visualizer/scripts/visualize.py .
```

This creates `codebase-map.html` in the current directory and opens it in your default browser.

## What the visualization shows

- **Collapsible directories**: Click folders to expand/collapse
- **File sizes**: Displayed next to each file
- **Colors**: Different colors for different file types
- **Directory totals**: Shows aggregate size of each folder
````

Create `~/.claude/skills/codebase-visualizer/scripts/visualize.py`. This script scans a directory tree and generates a self-contained HTML file with:

* A **summary sidebar** showing file count, directory count, total size, and number of file types
* A **bar chart** breaking down the codebase by file type (top 8 by size)
* A **collapsible tree** where you can expand and collapse directories, with color-coded file type indicators

The script requires Python but uses only built-in libraries, so there are no packages to install:

```python expandable theme={null}
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate an interactive collapsible tree visualization of a codebase."""

import json
import sys
import webbrowser
from pathlib import Path
from collections import Counter

IGNORE = {'.git', 'node_modules', '__pycache__', '.venv', 'venv', 'dist', 'build'}

def scan(path: Path, stats: dict) -> dict:
    result = {"name": path.name, "children": [], "size": 0}
    try:
        for item in sorted(path.iterdir()):
            if item.name in IGNORE or item.name.startswith('.'):
                continue
            if item.is_file():
                size = item.stat().st_size
                ext = item.suffix.lower() or '(no ext)'
                result["children"].append({"name": item.name, "size": size, "ext": ext})
                result["size"] += size
                stats["files"] += 1
                stats["extensions"][ext] += 1
                stats["ext_sizes"][ext] += size
            elif item.is_dir():
                stats["dirs"] += 1
                child = scan(item, stats)
                if child["children"]:
                    result["children"].append(child)
                    result["size"] += child["size"]
    except PermissionError:
        pass
    return result

def generate_html(data: dict, stats: dict, output: Path) -> None:
    ext_sizes = stats["ext_sizes"]
    total_size = sum(ext_sizes.values()) or 1
    sorted_exts = sorted(ext_sizes.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:8]
    colors = {
        '.js': '#f7df1e', '.ts': '#3178c6', '.py': '#3776ab', '.go': '#00add8',
        '.rs': '#dea584', '.rb': '#cc342d', '.css': '#264de4', '.html': '#e34c26',
        '.json': '#6b7280', '.md': '#083fa1', '.yaml': '#cb171e', '.yml': '#cb171e',
        '.mdx': '#083fa1', '.tsx': '#3178c6', '.jsx': '#61dafb', '.sh': '#4eaa25',
    }
    lang_bars = "".join(
        f'<div class="bar-row"><span class="bar-label">{ext}</span>'
        f'<div class="bar" style="width:{(size/total_size)*100}%;background:{colors.get(ext,"#6b7280")}"></div>'
        f'<span class="bar-pct">{(size/total_size)*100:.1f}%</span></div>'
        for ext, size in sorted_exts
    )
    def fmt(b):
        if b < 1024: return f"{b} B"
        if b < 1048576: return f"{b/1024:.1f} KB"
        return f"{b/1048576:.1f} MB"

    html = f'''<!DOCTYPE html>
<html><head>
  <meta charset="utf-8"><title>Codebase Explorer</title>
  <style>
    body {{ font: 14px/1.5 system-ui, sans-serif; margin: 0; background: #1a1a2e; color: #eee; }}
    .container {{ display: flex; height: 100vh; }}
    .sidebar {{ width: 280px; background: #252542; padding: 20px; border-right: 1px solid #3d3d5c; overflow-y: auto; flex-shrink: 0; }}
    .main {{ flex: 1; padding: 20px; overflow-y: auto; }}
    h1 {{ margin: 0 0 10px 0; font-size: 18px; }}
    h2 {{ margin: 20px 0 10px 0; font-size: 14px; color: #888; text-transform: uppercase; }}
    .stat {{ display: flex; justify-content: space-between; padding: 8px 0; border-bottom: 1px solid #3d3d5c; }}
    .stat-value {{ font-weight: bold; }}
    .bar-row {{ display: flex; align-items: center; margin: 6px 0; }}
    .bar-label {{ width: 55px; font-size: 12px; color: #aaa; }}
    .bar {{ height: 18px; border-radius: 3px; }}
    .bar-pct {{ margin-left: 8px; font-size: 12px; color: #666; }}
    .tree {{ list-style: none; padding-left: 20px; }}
    details {{ cursor: pointer; }}
    summary {{ padding: 4px 8px; border-radius: 4px; }}
    summary:hover {{ background: #2d2d44; }}
    .folder {{ color: #ffd700; }}
    .file {{ display: flex; align-items: center; padding: 4px 8px; border-radius: 4px; }}
    .file:hover {{ background: #2d2d44; }}
    .size {{ color: #888; margin-left: auto; font-size: 12px; }}
    .dot {{ width: 8px; height: 8px; border-radius: 50%; margin-right: 8px; }}
  </style>
</head><body>
  <div class="container">
    <div class="sidebar">
      <h1>📊 Summary</h1>
      <div class="stat"><span>Files</span><span class="stat-value">{stats["files"]:,}</span></div>
      <div class="stat"><span>Directories</span><span class="stat-value">{stats["dirs"]:,}</span></div>
      <div class="stat"><span>Total size</span><span class="stat-value">{fmt(data["size"])}</span></div>
      <div class="stat"><span>File types</span><span class="stat-value">{len(stats["extensions"])}</span></div>
      <h2>By file type</h2>
      {lang_bars}
    </div>
    <div class="main">
      <h1>📁 {data["name"]}</h1>
      <ul class="tree" id="root"></ul>
    </div>
  </div>
  <script>
    const data = {json.dumps(data)};
    const colors = {json.dumps(colors)};
    function fmt(b) {{ if (b < 1024) return b + ' B'; if (b < 1048576) return (b/1024).toFixed(1) + ' KB'; return (b/1048576).toFixed(1) + ' MB'; }}
    function render(node, parent) {{
      if (node.children) {{
        const det = document.createElement('details');
        det.open = parent === document.getElementById('root');
        det.innerHTML = `<summary><span class="folder">📁 ${{node.name}}</span><span class="size">${{fmt(node.size)}}</span></summary>`;
        const ul = document.createElement('ul'); ul.className = 'tree';
        node.children.sort((a,b) => (b.children?1:0)-(a.children?1:0) || a.name.localeCompare(b.name));
        node.children.forEach(c => render(c, ul));
        det.appendChild(ul);
        const li = document.createElement('li'); li.appendChild(det); parent.appendChild(li);
      }} else {{
        const li = document.createElement('li'); li.className = 'file';
        li.innerHTML = `<span class="dot" style="background:${{colors[node.ext]||'#6b7280'}}"></span>${{node.name}}<span class="size">${{fmt(node.size)}}</span>`;
        parent.appendChild(li);
      }}
    }}
    data.children.forEach(c => render(c, document.getElementById('root')));
  </script>
</body></html>'''
    output.write_text(html)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    target = Path(sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else '.').resolve()
    stats = {"files": 0, "dirs": 0, "extensions": Counter(), "ext_sizes": Counter()}
    data = scan(target, stats)
    out = Path('codebase-map.html')
    generate_html(data, stats, out)
    print(f'Generated {out.absolute()}')
    webbrowser.open(f'file://{out.absolute()}')
```

To test, open Claude Code in any project and ask "Visualize this codebase." Claude runs the script, generates `codebase-map.html`, and opens it in your browser.

This pattern works for any visual output: dependency graphs, test coverage reports, API documentation, or database schema visualizations. The bundled script does the heavy lifting while Claude handles orchestration.

## Troubleshooting

### Skill not triggering

If Claude doesn't use your skill when expected:

1. Check the description includes keywords users would naturally say
2. Verify the skill appears in `What skills are available?`
3. Try rephrasing your request to match the description more closely
4. Invoke it directly with `/skill-name` if the skill is user-invocable

### Skill triggers too often

If Claude uses your skill when you don't want it:

1. Make the description more specific
2. Add `disable-model-invocation: true` if you only want manual invocation

### Claude doesn't see all my skills

Skill descriptions are loaded into context so Claude knows what's available. If you have many skills, they may exceed the character budget. The budget scales dynamically at 2% of the context window, with a fallback of 16,000 characters. Run `/context` to check for a warning about excluded skills.

To override the limit, set the `SLASH_COMMAND_TOOL_CHAR_BUDGET` environment variable.

## Related resources

* **[Subagents](/en/sub-agents)**: delegate tasks to specialized agents
* **[Plugins](/en/plugins)**: package and distribute skills with other extensions
* **[Hooks](/en/hooks)**: automate workflows around tool events
* **[Memory](/en/memory)**: manage CLAUDE.md files for persistent context
* **[Interactive mode](/en/interactive-mode#built-in-commands)**: built-in commands and shortcuts
* **[Permissions](/en/permissions)**: control tool and skill access
