1→--- 2→name: react-email 3→description: Use when creating HTML email templates with React components - welcome emails, password resets, notifications, order confirmations, newsletters, or transactional emails. 4→license: MIT 5→metadata: 6→ author: Resend 7→ version: "1.1.0" 8→--- 9→ 10→# React Email 11→ 12→Build and send HTML emails using React components - a modern, component-based approach to email development that works across all major email clients. 13→ 14→## Installation 15→ 16→You need to scaffold a new React Email project using the create-email CLI. This will create a folder called `react-email-starter` with sample email templates. 17→ 18→Using npm: 19→```sh 20→npx create-email@latest 21→``` 22→ 23→Using yarn: 24→```sh 25→yarn create email 26→``` 27→ 28→Using pnpm: 29→```sh 30→pnpm create email 31→``` 32→ 33→Using bun: 34→```sh 35→bun create email 36→``` 37→ 38→## Navigate to Project Directory 39→ 40→You must change into the newly created project folder: 41→ 42→```sh 43→cd react-email-starter 44→``` 45→ 46→## Install Dependencies 47→ 48→You need to install all project dependencies before running the development server. 49→ 50→Using npm: 51→```sh 52→npm install 53→``` 54→ 55→Using yarn: 56→```sh 57→yarn 58→``` 59→ 60→Using pnpm: 61→```sh 62→pnpm install 63→``` 64→ 65→Using bun: 66→```sh 67→bun install 68→``` 69→ 70→## Start the Development Server 71→ 72→Your task is to start the local preview server to view and edit email templates. 73→ 74→Using npm: 75→```sh 76→npm run dev 77→``` 78→ 79→Using yarn: 80→```sh 81→yarn dev 82→``` 83→ 84→Using pnpm: 85→```sh 86→pnpm dev 87→``` 88→ 89→Using bun: 90→```sh 91→bun dev 92→``` 93→ 94→## Verify Installation 95→ 96→Confirm the development server is running by checking that localhost:3000 is accessible. The server will display a preview interface where you can view email templates from the `emails` folder. 97→ 98→### Notes on installation 99→Assuming React Email is installed in an existing project, update the top-level package.json file with a script to run the React Email preview server. 100→ 101→```json 102→{ 103→ "scripts": { 104→ "email": "email dev --dir emails --port 3000" 105→ } 106→} 107→``` 108→ 109→Make sure the path to the emails folder is relative to the base project directory. 110→ 111→ 112→### tsconfig.json updating or creation 113→ 114→Ensure the tsconfig.json includes proper support for jsx. 115→ 116→## Basic Email Template 117→ 118→Replace the sample email templates. Here is how to create a new email template: 119→ 120→Create an email component with proper structure using the Tailwind component for styling: 121→ 122→```tsx 123→import { 124→ Html, 125→ Head, 126→ Preview, 127→ Body, 128→ Container, 129→ Heading, 130→ Text, 131→ Button, 132→ Tailwind, 133→ pixelBasedPreset 134→} from '@react-email/components'; 135→ 136→interface WelcomeEmailProps { 137→ name: string; 138→ verificationUrl: string; 139→} 140→ 141→export default function WelcomeEmail({ name, verificationUrl }: WelcomeEmailProps) { 142→ return ( 143→ 144→ 156→ 157→ Welcome - Verify your email 158→ 159→ 160→ 161→ Welcome! 162→ 163→ 164→ Hi {name}, thanks for signing up! 165→ 166→ 172→ 173→ 174→ 175→ 176→ ); 177→} 178→ 179→// Preview props for testing 180→WelcomeEmail.PreviewProps = { 181→ name: 'John Doe', 182→ verificationUrl: 'https://example.com/verify/abc123' 183→} satisfies WelcomeEmailProps; 184→ 185→export { WelcomeEmail }; 186→``` 187→ 188→## Essential Components 189→ 190→See [references/COMPONENTS.md](references/COMPONENTS.md) for complete component documentation. 191→ 192→**Core Structure:** 193→- `Html` - Root wrapper with `lang` attribute 194→- `Head` - Meta elements, styles, fonts 195→- `Body` - Main content wrapper 196→- `Container` - Centers content (max-width layout) 197→- `Section` - Layout sections 198→- `Row` & `Column` - Multi-column layouts 199→- `Tailwind` - Enables Tailwind CSS utility classes 200→ 201→**Content:** 202→- `Preview` - Inbox preview text, always first in `Body` 203→- `Heading` - h1-h6 headings 204→- `Text` - Paragraphs 205→- `Button` - Styled link buttons 206→- `Link` - Hyperlinks 207→- `Img` - Images (see Static Files section below) 208→- `Hr` - Horizontal dividers 209→ 210→**Specialized:** 211→- `CodeBlock` - Syntax-highlighted code 212→- `CodeInline` - Inline code 213→- `Markdown` - Render markdown 214→- `Font` - Custom web fonts 215→ 216→## Before Writing Code 217→ 218→When a user requests an email template, ask clarifying questions FIRST if they haven't provided: 219→ 220→1. **Brand colors** - Ask for primary brand color (hex code like #007bff) 221→2. **Logo** - Ask if they have a logo file and its format (PNG/JPG only - warn if SVG/WEBP) 222→3. **Style preference** - Professional, casual, or minimal tone 223→4. **Production URL** - Where will static assets be hosted in production? 224→ 225→Example response to vague request: 226→> Before I create your email template, I have a few questions: 227→> 1. What is your primary brand color? (hex code) 228→> 2. Do you have a logo file? (PNG or JPG - note: SVG and WEBP don't work reliably in email clients) 229→> 3. What tone do you prefer - professional, casual, or minimal? 230→> 4. Where will you host static assets in production? (e.g., https://cdn.example.com) 231→ 232→## Static Files and Images 233→ 234→### Directory Structure 235→ 236→Local images must be placed in the `static` folder inside your emails directory: 237→ 238→``` 239→project/ 240→├── emails/ 241→│ ├── welcome.tsx 242→│ └── static/ <-- Images go here 243→│ └── logo.png 244→``` 245→ 246→If user has an image elsewhere, instruct them to copy it: 247→```sh 248→cp ./assets/logo.png ./emails/static/logo.png 249→``` 250→ 251→### Dev vs Production URLs 252→ 253→Use this pattern for images that work in both dev preview and production: 254→ 255→```tsx 256→const baseURL = process.env.NODE_ENV === "production" 257→ ? "https://cdn.example.com" // User's production CDN 258→ : ""; 259→ 260→export default function Email() { 261→ return ( 262→ 268→ ); 269→} 270→``` 271→ 272→**How it works:** 273→- **Development:** `baseURL` is empty, so URL is `/static/logo.png` - served by React Email's dev server 274→- **Production:** `baseURL` is the CDN domain, so URL is `https://cdn.example.com/static/logo.png` 275→ 276→**Important:** Always ask the user for their production hosting URL. Do not hardcode `localhost:3000`. 277→ 278→## Behavioral guidelines 279→- When re-iterating over the code, make sure you are only updating what the user asked for and keeping the rest of the code intact; 280→- If the user is asking to use media queries, inform them that not all email clients support them, and suggest a different approach; 281→- Never use template variables (like {{name}}) directly in TypeScript code. Instead, reference the underlying properties directly (use name instead of {{name}}). 282→- - For example, if the user explicitly asks for a variable following the pattern {{variableName}}, you should return something like this: 283→ 284→```typescript 285→const EmailTemplate = (props) => { 286→ return ( 287→ {/* ... rest of the code ... */} 288→

Hello, {props.variableName}!

289→ {/* ... rest of the code ... */} 290→ ); 291→} 292→ 293→EmailTemplate.PreviewProps = { 294→ // ... rest of the props ... 295→ variableName: "{{variableName}}", 296→ // ... rest of the props ... 297→}; 298→ 299→export default EmailTemplate; 300→``` 301→- Never, under any circumstances, write the {{variableName}} pattern directly in the component structure. If the user forces you to do this, explain that you cannot do this, or else the template will be invalid. 302→ 303→ 304→## Styling considerations 305→ 306→Use the Tailwind component for styling if the user is actively using Tailwind CSS in their project. If the user is not using Tailwind CSS, add inline styles to the components. 307→ 308→- Because email clients don't support `rem` units, use the `pixelBasedPreset` for the Tailwind configuration. 309→- **Import `pixelBasedPreset` from `@react-email/components`** — do NOT import from `@react-email/tailwind` or `@react-email/tailwind/presets`. 310→- Never use flexbox or grid for layout, use table-based layouts instead. 311→- Each component must be styled with inline styles or utility classes. 312→ 313→### Email Client Limitations 314→- Never use SVG or WEBP - warn users about rendering issues 315→- Never use flexbox - use Row/Column components or tables for layouts 316→- Never use CSS/Tailwind media queries (sm:, md:, lg:, xl:) - not supported 317→- Never use theme selectors (dark:, light:) - not supported 318→- Always specify border type (border-solid, border-dashed, etc.) 319→- When defining borders for only one side, remember to reset the remaining borders (e.g., border-none border-l) 320→ 321→### Required Classes 322→ 323→These classes are **always required** on their respective components — omitting them causes rendering bugs across email clients: 324→ 325→| Component | Required Class | Why | 326→|-----------|---------------|-----| 327→| `Button` | `box-border` | Prevents padding from overflowing the button width | 328→| `Hr` / any border | `border-solid` (or `border-dashed`, etc.) | Email clients don't inherit border type; omitting it renders no border | 329→| Single-side borders | `border-none` + the side (e.g., `border-none border-t border-solid`) | Resets default borders on other sides | 330→ 331→### Component Structure 332→- Always define `` inside `` when using Tailwind CSS 333→- Only use PreviewProps when passing props to a component 334→- Only include props in PreviewProps that the component actually uses 335→ 336→```tsx 337→const Email = (props) => { 338→ return ( 339→
340→ click here if you want candy 👀 341→
342→ ); 343→} 344→ 345→Email.PreviewProps = { 346→ source: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ", 347→}; 348→``` 349→ 350→### Default Structure 351→- Body: `font-sans py-10 bg-gray-100` 352→- Container: white, centered, content left-aligned 353→- Footer: physical address, unsubscribe link, current year with `m-0` on address/copyright 354→ 355→### Typography 356→- Titles: bold, larger font, larger margins 357→- Paragraphs: regular weight, smaller font, smaller margins 358→- Use consistent spacing respecting content hierarchy 359→ 360→### Images 361→- Only include if user requests 362→- Never use fixed width/height - use responsive units (w-full, h-auto) 363→- Never distort user-provided images 364→- Never create SVG images - only use provided or web images 365→ 366→### Buttons 367→- Always use `box-border` to prevent padding overflow 368→ 369→### Layout 370→- Always mobile-friendly by default 371→- Use stacked layouts that work on all screen sizes 372→- Remove default spacing/margins/padding between list items 373→ 374→### Dark Mode 375→When requested: container black (#000), background dark gray (#151516) 376→ 377→### Best Practices 378→- Choose colors, layout, and copy based on user's request 379→- Make templates unique, not generic 380→- Use keywords in email body to increase conversion 381→ 382→## Rendering 383→ 384→### Convert to HTML 385→ 386→```tsx 387→import { render } from '@react-email/components'; 388→import { WelcomeEmail } from './emails/welcome'; 389→ 390→const html = await render( 391→ 392→); 393→``` 394→ 395→### Convert to Plain Text 396→ 397→```tsx 398→import { render } from '@react-email/components'; 399→import { WelcomeEmail } from './emails/welcome'; 400→ 401→const text = await render(, { plainText: true }); 402→``` 403→ 404→## Sending 405→ 406→React Email supports sending with any email service provider. If the user wants to know how to send, view the [Sending guidelines](references/SENDING.md). 407→ 408→Quick example using the Resend SDK for Node.js: 409→ 410→```tsx 411→import { Resend } from 'resend'; 412→import { WelcomeEmail } from './emails/welcome'; 413→ 414→const resend = new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY); 415→ 416→const { data, error } = await resend.emails.send({ 417→ from: 'Acme ', 418→ to: ['user@example.com'], 419→ subject: 'Welcome to Acme', 420→ react: 421→}); 422→ 423→if (error) { 424→ console.error('Failed to send:', error); 425→} 426→``` 427→ 428→The Node SDK automatically handles the plain-text rendering and HTML rendering for you. 429→ 430→## Internationalization 431→ 432→See [references/I18N.md](references/I18N.md) for complete i18n documentation. 433→ 434→React Email supports three i18n libraries: next-intl, react-i18next, and react-intl. 435→ 436→### Quick Example (next-intl) 437→ 438→```tsx 439→import { createTranslator } from 'next-intl'; 440→import { 441→ Html, 442→ Body, 443→ Container, 444→ Text, 445→ Button, 446→ Tailwind, 447→ pixelBasedPreset 448→} from '@react-email/components'; 449→ 450→interface EmailProps { 451→ name: string; 452→ locale: string; 453→} 454→ 455→export default async function WelcomeEmail({ name, locale }: EmailProps) { 456→ const t = createTranslator({ 457→ messages: await import(\`../messages/\${locale}.json\`), 458→ namespace: 'welcome-email', 459→ locale 460→ }); 461→ 462→ return ( 463→ 464→ 465→ 466→ 467→ {t('greeting')} {name}, 468→ {t('body')} 469→ 472→ 473→ 474→ 475→ 476→ ); 477→} 478→``` 479→ 480→Message files (\`messages/en.json\`, \`messages/es.json\`, etc.): 481→ 482→```json 483→{ 484→ "welcome-email": { 485→ "greeting": "Hi", 486→ "body": "Thanks for signing up!", 487→ "cta": "Get Started" 488→ } 489→} 490→``` 491→ 492→## Email Best Practices 493→ 494→1. **Test across email clients** - Test in Gmail, Outlook, Apple Mail, Yahoo Mail. Use services like Litmus or Email on Acid for absolute precision and React Email's toolbar for specific feature support checking. 495→ 496→2. **Keep it responsive** - Max-width around 600px, test on mobile devices. 497→ 498→3. **Use absolute image URLs** - Host on reliable CDN, always include \`alt\` text. 499→ 500→4. **Provide plain text version** - Required for accessibility and some email clients. 501→ 502→5. **Keep file size under 102KB** - Gmail clips larger emails. 503→ 504→6. **Add proper TypeScript types** - Define interfaces for all email props. 505→ 506→7. **Include preview props** - Add \`.PreviewProps\` to components for development testing. 507→ 508→8. **Handle errors** - Always check for errors when sending emails. 509→ 510→9. **Use verified domains** - For production, use verified domains in \`from\` addresses. 511→ 512→## Common Patterns 513→ 514→See [references/PATTERNS.md](references/PATTERNS.md) for complete examples including: 515→- Password reset emails 516→- Order confirmations with product lists 517→- Notification emails with code blocks 518→- Multi-column layouts 519→- Email templates with custom fonts 520→ 521→## Additional Resources 522→ 523→- [React Email Documentation](https://react.email/docs/llms.txt) 524→- [React Email GitHub](https://github.com/resend/react-email) 525→- [Resend Documentation](https://resend.com/docs/llms.txt) 526→- [Email Client CSS Support](https://www.caniemail.com) 527→- Component Reference: [references/COMPONENTS.md](references/COMPONENTS.md) 528→- Internationalization Guide: [references/I18N.md](references/I18N.md) 529→- Common Patterns: [references/PATTERNS.md](references/PATTERNS.md) 530→

content.content:
1→---
     2→name: e2b-sandbox
     3→description: Guide for creating and managing E2B sandboxes using ComputeSDK. Use when building applications that need E2B Firecracker microVM sandboxes for secure code execution, AI code runners, or isolated development environments on E2B.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# E2B Sandboxes with ComputeSDK
     7→
     8→Run code in E2B's Firecracker microVMs through ComputeSDK's unified API. E2B provides sub-second cold starts and secure isolation — ideal for AI code execution, data science, and educational platforms.
     9→
    10→## Setup
    11→
    12→```bash
    13→npm install computesdk
    14→```
    15→
    16→```bash
    17→# .env
    18→COMPUTESDK_API_KEY=your_computesdk_api_key
    19→E2B_API_KEY=your_e2b_api_key
    20→```
    21→
    22→Get your ComputeSDK key at https://console.computesdk.com/register
    23→
    24→## Quick Start
    25→
    26→```typescript
    27→import { compute } from 'computesdk';
    28→// Auto-detects E2B from environment variables
    29→
    30→const sandbox = await compute.sandbox.create();
    31→
    32→const result = await sandbox.runCode('print("Hello from E2B!")');
    33→console.log(result.output);
    34→
    35→await sandbox.destroy();
    36→```
    37→
    38→## Explicit Configuration
    39→
    40→For multi-provider setups or when you want to be explicit:
    41→
    42→```typescript
    43→import { compute } from 'computesdk';
    44→
    45→compute.setConfig({
    46→  computesdkApiKey: process.env.COMPUTESDK_API_KEY,
    47→  provider: 'e2b',
    48→  e2b: {
    49→    apiKey: process.env.E2B_API_KEY,
    50→  }
    51→});
    52→
    53→const sandbox = await compute.sandbox.create();
    54→```
    55→
    56→## E2B Configuration Options
    57→
    58→```typescript
    59→interface E2BConfig {
    60→  apiKey?: string;              // Uses E2B_API_KEY env var if not set
    61→  runtime?: 'node' | 'python'; // Auto-detects from code patterns
    62→  timeout?: number;             // Execution timeout in ms
    63→}
    64→```
    65→
    66→## Runtime Detection
    67→
    68→E2B auto-detects Python from `print` statements, `import`, `def`, and Python-specific syntax like `f"strings"`. All other code defaults to Node.js.
    69→
    70→## Full API
    71→
    72→ComputeSDK provides the same API across all providers: filesystem operations, shell commands, managed servers, overlays, terminals, and client access.
    73→
    74→Install the main skill for the complete reference:
    75→
    76→```
    77→npx skills add https://github.com/computesdk/sandbox-skills --skill computesdk
    78→```
    79→
    80→Or see https://www.computesdk.com/docs/reference/sandbox/

content.content:
---
name: better-auth-best-practices
description: Skill for integrating Better Auth - the comprehensive TypeScript authentication framework.
---

# Better Auth Integration Guide

**Always consult [better-auth.com/docs](https://better-auth.com/docs) for code examples and latest API.**

Better Auth is a TypeScript-first, framework-agnostic auth framework supporting email/password, OAuth, magic links, passkeys, and more via plugins.

---

## Quick Reference

### Environment Variables
- `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` - Encryption secret (min 32 chars). Generate: `openssl rand -base64 32`
- `BETTER_AUTH_URL` - Base URL (e.g., `https://example.com`)

Only define `baseURL`/`secret` in config if env vars are NOT set.

### File Location
CLI looks for `auth.ts` in: `./`, `./lib`, `./utils`, or under `./src`. Use `--config` for custom path.

### CLI Commands
- `npx @better-auth/cli@latest migrate` - Apply schema (built-in adapter)
- `npx @better-auth/cli@latest generate` - Generate schema for Prisma/Drizzle
- `npx @better-auth/cli mcp --cursor` - Add MCP to AI tools

**Re-run after adding/changing plugins.**

1→---
     2→name: better-auth-best-practices
     3→description: Skill for integrating Better Auth - the comprehensive TypeScript authentication framework.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Better Auth Integration Guide
     7→
     8→**Always consult [better-auth.com/docs](https://better-auth.com/docs) for code examples and latest API.**
     9→
    10→Better Auth is a TypeScript-first, framework-agnostic auth framework supporting email/password, OAuth, magic links, passkeys, and more via plugins.
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Quick Reference
    15→
    16→### Environment Variables
    17→- `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` - Encryption secret (min 32 chars). Generate: `openssl rand -base64 32`
    18→- `BETTER_AUTH_URL` - Base URL (e.g., `https://example.com`)
    19→
    20→Only define `baseURL`/`secret` in config if env vars are NOT set.
    21→
    22→### File Location
    23→CLI looks for `auth.ts` in: `./`, `./lib`, `./utils`, or under `./src`. Use `--config` for custom path.
    24→
    25→### CLI Commands
    26→- `npx @better-auth/cli@latest migrate` - Apply schema (built-in adapter)
    27→- `npx @better-auth/cli@latest generate` - Generate schema for Prisma/Drizzle
    28→- `npx @better-auth/cli mcp --cursor` - Add MCP to AI tools
    29→
    30→**Re-run after adding/changing plugins.**
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## Core Config Options
    35→
    36→| Option | Notes |
    37→|--------|-------|
    38→| `appName` | Optional display name |
    39→| `baseURL` | Only if `BETTER_AUTH_URL` not set |
    40→| `basePath` | Default `/api/auth`. Set `/` for root. |
    41→| `secret` | Only if `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` not set |
    42→| `database` | Required for most features. See adapters docs. |
    43→| `secondaryStorage` | Redis/KV for sessions & rate limits |
    44→| `emailAndPassword` | `{ enabled: true }` to activate |
    45→| `socialProviders` | `{ google: { clientId, clientSecret }, ... }` |
    46→| `plugins` | Array of plugins |
    47→| `trustedOrigins` | CSRF whitelist |
    48→
    49→---
    50→

<result>Perfect! I have a comprehensive understanding of the skills structure. Let me compile my findings.

## Skills Structure Summary

### Key Locations:

1. **Project Skills Directory**: `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/skills/`
   - Contains symlinks to actual skill implementations in `.agents/skills/`

2. **Actual Skills Location**: `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/.agents/skills/`
   - Contains the actual skill directories from external GitHub sources

3. **Skills Lock File**: `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/skills-lock.json`
   - Tracks skill versions and integrity hashes
   - Sources are GitHub repositories with computed hashes for version control

### Registered Skills (from skills-lock.json):

| Skill | Source | Type |
|-------|--------|------|
| `better-auth-best-practices` | better-auth/skills | GitHub |
| `e2b-sandbox` | computesdk/sandbox-skills | GitHub |
| `email-best-practices` | resend/email-best-practices | GitHub |
| `react-email` | resend/react-email | GitHub |
| `resend` | resend/resend-skills | GitHub |

### Skill File Structure:

Each skill follows a consistent pattern:

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md                 # Main skill documentation (required)
│                            # Format: YAML frontmatter + markdown content
├── README.md               # Optional: overview/setup
├── package.json            # Optional: dependencies
├── TESTS.md               # Optional: test specifications
├── references/            # Optional: detailed reference docs
│   ├── COMPONENTS.md
│   ├── PATTERNS.md
│   ├── STYLING.md
│   └── ...
├── resources/             # Optional: resource materials
│   └── marketing-emails.md
└── [subdirectories]/      # For hierarchical skills (resend, react-email)
    ├── send-email/
    │   ├── SKILL.md
    │   └── references/
    ├── resend-inbound/
    │   └── SKILL.md
    └── agent-email-inbox/
        └── SKILL.md
```

### SKILL.md Format (Frontmatter):

```yaml
---
name: skill-name              # Unique identifier
description: "..."            # One-line description
license: MIT                  # Optional
metadata:                     # Optional
  author: author-name
  version: "x.x.x"
  homepage: https://...
  source: https://github.com/...
inputs:                      # Optional: environment variables
  - name: ENV_VAR_NAME
    description: "..."
    required: true|false
---
```

### Project Integration (from AGENTS.md):

- **Skills are external resources** imported from GitHub repositories
- **No custom project-specific skills** in the skills/ directory (only symlinks)
- **Skills are read-only** references for Claude Code/agents
- **Skills manage their own versions** via GitHub source control with hash verification

### Local Skills Directory (~/.claude/skills/):

The user also has local skills at `/Users/nishitbariya/.claude/skills/` containing:
- Symlinks to `.agents/skills/`
- Some standalone directories like `vercel-react-best-practices` and `web-design-guidelines`
- Additional skills like `ai-sdk`, `find-skills`, `next-best-practices`

### Convention:

Skills are **curated, external knowledge bases** that Claude uses to understand best practices, API patterns, and configuration details for specific technologies. They're designed to be:
- **Self-contained** (each has complete documentation)
- **Hierarchical** (complex features like Resend have sub-skills)
- **Versioned** (via GitHub with integrity hashes)
- **Searchable** (via markdown reference sections)</result>

<summary>Agent "Research skill file format" completed</summary>
<result>Excellent! Now I have the complete picture. Let me provide you with comprehensive answers to all your questions:

## Custom Skills in Claude Code: Comprehensive Guide

Based on the official Claude Code documentation, here are detailed answers to your questions:

### 1. File Format for Skill .md Files

Skills use a **SKILL.md** file with two parts:

**YAML Frontmatter** (between `---` markers):
```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: What this skill does and when to use it
disable-model-invocation: true  # optional
allowed-tools: Read, Grep       # optional
context: fork                   # optional
---
```

**Markdown Content**:
Instructions and reference material that Claude follows when the skill is invoked.

Example skill structure:
```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md           # Main instructions (required)
├── template.md        # Supporting files (optional)
├── examples/
│   └── sample.md
└── scripts/
    └── validate.sh
```

### 2. Where Skills Are Placed

Skills work at multiple scopes with a precedence hierarchy:

| Location | Path | Scope | Priority |
|----------|------|-------|----------|
| Enterprise | Managed settings | All users in organization | Highest |
| Personal | `~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` | All your projects | Medium |
| Project | `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` | This project only | Lower |
| Plugin | `<plugin>/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` | Where plugin enabled | Plugin namespace |

When skills share the same name across levels, higher-priority locations win: **enterprise > personal > project**.

Claude Code also automatically discovers skills from nested `.claude/skills/` directories in subdirectories, which is useful for monorepo setups where packages have their own skills.

### 3. How Skills Are Invoked

Users invoke skills in three ways:

**Direct invocation** with slash command:
```
/skill-name
/skill-name with arguments
```

**Claude auto-invocation** when relevant to conversation (based on the `description` field).

**Skill descriptions** are always loaded into context so Claude knows what's available, but full skill content only loads when invoked.

### 4. Structure of skills-lock.json

This is a lockfile that tracks installed skills from external sources. Based on your project file at `/Users/nishitbariya/Github/02-Web-Apps/runagents/skills-lock.json`:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "skills": {
    "skill-name": {
      "source": "github-username/repository-name",
      "sourceType": "github",
      "computedHash": "sha256-hash-for-integrity-verification"
    }
  }
}
```

**Fields explained:**
- `version`: Format version for the lock file
- `skills`: Object containing each installed skill
  - `source`: Repository path (e.g., `resend/email-best-practices`)
  - `sourceType`: Source platform (currently `github`)
  - `computedHash`: SHA-256 hash for integrity verification and change detection

This lockfile serves similar purposes to `package-lock.json` or `yarn.lock` - it ensures reproducible skill installations and tracks versions/sources.

### 5. Frontmatter Fields and Metadata

Essential frontmatter fields for `SKILL.md`:

| Field | Required | Purpose |
|-------|----------|---------|
| `name` | No | Skill display name and `/slash-command`. Lowercase, hyphens, max 64 chars |
| `description` | Recommended | When to use the skill. Claude uses this to decide automatic invocation |
| `disable-model-invocation` | No | Set to `true` to prevent Claude auto-invocation (manual only) |
| `user-invocable` | No | Set to `false` to hide from `/` menu (Claude-only reference) |
| `allowed-tools` | No | Tools Claude can use without permission when skill active |
| `argument-hint` | No | Hint for autocomplete, e.g., `[issue-number]` |
| `model` | No | Model to use when skill is active |
| `context` | No | Set to `fork` to run in isolated subagent |
| `agent` | No | Which subagent type to use with `context: fork` |
| `hooks` | No | Hooks scoped to skill lifecycle |

**Dynamic string substitutions** available in skill content:
- `$ARGUMENTS` - All arguments passed
- `$ARGUMENTS[N]` or `$N` - Specific argument by index
- `${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}` - Current session ID

**Example with substitutions:**
```yaml
---
name: fix-issue
description: Fix a GitHub issue
---
Fix GitHub issue $ARGUMENTS following our standards.
```

When invoked as `/fix-issue 123`, Claude sees "Fix GitHub issue 123..."

---

### Additional Notes

From your project, I can see you have several installed skills from GitHub repos (better-auth, e2b-sandbox, email-best-practices, react-email, resend). These are tracked in your skills-lock.json file.

For best practices, keep `SKILL.md` under 500 lines and move detailed reference material to supporting files. Skills can also bundle scripts in any language that Claude can execute to generate visual output or perform complex operations.

Sources:
- [Extend Claude with skills - Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md)
- [Claude Code documentation map](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md)</result>

file.content:
---
name: web-design-guidelines
description: Review UI code for Web Interface Guidelines compliance. Use when asked to "review my UI", "check accessibility", "audit design", "review UX", or "check my site against best practices".
metadata:
  author: vercel
  version: "1.0.0"
  argument-hint: <file-or-pattern>
---

# Web Interface Guidelines

Review files for compliance with Web Interface Guidelines.

## How It Works

1. Fetch the latest guidelines from the source URL below
2. Read the specified files (or prompt user for files/pattern)
3. Check against all rules in the fetched guidelines
4. Output findings in the terse `file:line` format

## Guidelines Source

Fetch fresh guidelines before each review:

```
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vercel-labs/web-interface-guidelines/main/command.md
```

Use WebFetch to retrieve the latest rules. The fetched content contains all the rules and output format instructions.

## Usage

When a user provides a file or pattern argument:
1. Fetch guidelines from the source URL above
2. Read the specified files
3. Apply all rules from the fetched guidelines
4. Output findings using the format specified in the guidelines

If no files specified, ask the user which files to review.

input.content:
---
name: ship-it
description: Review code quality, run linting/type-checks, fix errors, create a feature branch synced with development, commit, push, and open a PR. Use when asked to "ship it", "push my changes", "create a PR", "review and push", or "finalize and submit".
metadata:
  author: nishitbariya
  version: "1.0.0"
argument-hint: [branch-name] [pr-title]
---

# Ship It — Review, Lint, Branch, Push & PR

Automated pipeline that reviews code, fixes issues, creates a branch, pushes, and opens a PR. Execute every step in order. Do NOT skip steps.

## Arguments

- `$1` — (optional) Branch name suffix. Default: auto-generated from changed files.
- `$2` — (optional) PR title. Default: auto-generated from commit analysis.

## Step 1 — Analyze Changes

1. Run `git status` to see all modified, staged, and untracked files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to understand what changed.
3. If there are NO changes at all (clean working tree), stop and tell the user "Nothing to ship — working tree is clean."

## Step 2 — Code Review

1. Read every changed/new file identified in Step 1.
2. Check for:
   - Obvious bugs or logic errors
   - Security issues (hardcoded secrets, injection vectors, exposed API keys)
   - Missing error handling at system boundaries
   - Unused imports or dead code you introduced
3. If you find issues, fix them immediately. Tell the user what you fixed and why.

## Step 3 — Linting

1. Detect the project's linter from `package.json` scripts (biome, eslint, prettier, etc.).
2. Run the lint command ONLY on changed files. Examples:
   - Biome: `pnpm lint -- <files>`
   - ESLint: `pnpm lint <files>`
3. If lint errors are found:
   - Run the formatter first (`pnpm format -- <files>` or equivalent).
   - Re-run lint. If errors remain, fix them manually.
   - Repeat until lint passes clean.

## Step 4 — Type Check

1. Run `pnpm type-check` or `npx tsc --noEmit` (whichever exists in scripts).
2. If TypeScript errors exist:
   - Fix errors that are clearly caused by the current changes.
   - If errors are pre-existing (not from your changes), note them but do NOT block the pipeline.
3. Confirm type-check passes or document pre-existing issues.

## Step 5 — Build Check (optional but recommended)

1. If a `build` script exists, run `pnpm build`.
2. If build fails due to current changes, fix the issues.
3. If build fails due to missing env vars or external dependencies, note it and proceed.

## Step 6 — Branch Management

1. Run `git branch --show-current` to check the current branch.
2. Run `git fetch origin` to sync remote state.
3. **If on `development` (or `main`/`master`):**
   - Create a new branch: `git checkout -b feature/<branch-name>`
   - Branch name: use `$1` if provided, otherwise derive from the changes (e.g., `feature/update-blog-components`).
   - The new branch is already based on the current development HEAD — no extra sync needed.
4. **If already on a feature branch:**
   - Stay on it. Pull latest from its upstream if it has one.
   - Rebase on development if behind: `git rebase origin/development`
   - Resolve conflicts if any arise (prefer current changes, ask user if ambiguous).

## Step 7 — Stage & Commit

1. Stage all relevant changed files. Use specific file paths — avoid `git add -A`.
   - Do NOT stage `.env`, `.env.local`, credentials, or secrets.
   - DO include lockfile changes if dependencies were added/updated.
2. Analyze all staged changes to write a commit message:
   - Follow Conventional Commits: `feat:`, `fix:`, `refactor:`, `docs:`, `chore:`, etc.
   - First line: concise summary under 72 chars.
   - Body: bullet points of what changed and why (if non-trivial).
   - End with: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>`
3. Commit using a HEREDOC for the message.

## Step 8 — Push

1. Push the branch to origin: `git push origin <branch-name>`
2. If push is rejected (branch exists remotely), use `git push origin <branch-name> --force-with-lease` — but ONLY with `--force-with-lease`, never `--force`.
3. Confirm push succeeded.

## Step 9 — Create Pull Request

1. Determine the base branch (usually `development`).
2. Run `git log origin/<base>..HEAD --oneline` to summarize all commits in the PR.
3. Create PR using GitHub CLI:

```
gh pr create \
  --base <base-branch> \
  --title "<pr-title>" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
<3-5 bullet points describing what changed and why>

## Changes
<list of key files changed with one-line descriptions>

## Testing
<how to verify these changes work>

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
```

4. Use `$2` as PR title if provided. Otherwise auto-generate from commit messages.
5. Output the PR URL to the user.

## Step 10 — Final Report

Output a summary:

```
✅ Ship complete!
Branch: <branch-name>
PR: <pr-url>
Commits: <count>
Files changed: <count>
Lint: passed
Types: passed / <note if pre-existing issues>
```

## Important Rules

- NEVER push directly to `development`, `main`, or `master`. Always create a feature branch.
- NEVER use `--force` push. Only `--force-with-lease` if absolutely needed.
- NEVER commit secrets or `.env` files.
- NEVER skip linting or type-checking. If they fail, fix first.
- If the user has uncommitted changes AND staged changes, commit them together unless they are clearly unrelated (then ask).
- Always use the project's package manager (check for `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `yarn.lock`, or `package-lock.json`).

content.text:
The skill is live — you can see **`ship-it`** is already registered in the skills list. Here's what it does when you invoke `/ship-it`:

### Pipeline (10 steps, fully automated):
1. **Analyze changes** — `git status`, `git diff`
2. **Code review** — reads every changed file, fixes bugs/security issues
3. **Lint** — runs your project's linter on changed files, auto-fixes
4. **Type check** — `pnpm type-check` / `tsc --noEmit`, fixes TS errors
5. **Build check** — `pnpm build` to catch build failures
6. **Branch management** — if on `development`, creates `feature/<name>` and checks out; if already on a feature branch, rebases on development
7. **Stage & commit** — stages files (never `.env`), writes conventional commit message
8. **Push** — pushes to origin
9. **Create PR** — opens a PR against `development` via `gh pr create`
10. **Final report** — summary with branch, PR URL, file count, lint/type status

### Usage:
```
/ship-it                              # auto-generates branch name + PR title
/ship-it update-navbar                # custom branch: feature/update-navbar
/ship-it update-navbar "fix: navbar"  # custom branch + PR title
```

Saved at `~/.claude/skills/ship-it/SKILL.md` — it's a personal skill, so it works across all your projects.

+# PRD: Basehub CMS Blog for RunAgents
+
+**Author**: Teammate 2 (PRD Writer)
+**Date**: 2026-02-25
+**Status**: Draft
+**Branch**: `development`
+
+---
+
+## 1. Objective
+
+Add a statically-generated blog to the RunAgents marketing site, powered by Basehub CMS. The blog will live under the `(landing)` route group at `/blog` and `/blog/[slug]`, following the same architectural patterns proven in the ShowCart reference project. Content will be managed entirely within the Basehub dashboard -- no local markdown files, no database tables.
+
+**Goals**:
+- Provide a public-facing blog for product updates, tutorials, and announcements.
+- Use Basehub CMS as the single source of truth for blog content.
+- Achieve full static generation (`force-static`) with `generateStaticParams` for post pages.
+- Produce proper SEO metadata (Open Graph, title, description) for every page.
+- Follow RunAgents file conventions (kebab-case files, PascalCase exports, `@/` alias, Biome linting).
+
+---
+
+## 2. Current State
+
+| Item | Status |
+|---|---|
+| `basehub` npm package | Installed (`^9.5.3` in `package.json`) |
+| Blog routes | None |
+| Blog components | None |
+| `BASEHUB_TOKEN` env var | Not configured anywhere |
+| `package.json` scripts | Standard `next dev` / `next build` -- missing `basehub` codegen prefix |
+| `.gitignore` entries for Basehub | Missing (`.basehub`, `basehub-types.d.ts`) |
+| Basehub dashboard schema | Not created |
+
+No blog-related code exists in the codebase. The `basehub` package was added as a dependency but never wired up.
+
+---
+
+## 3. Basehub Content Schema Required
+
+The following must be created **manually in the Basehub dashboard** before any code will function. This is a one-time setup task.
+\

This pull request implements a blog feature for the RunAgents marketing site, leveraging Basehub CMS for content management. It includes the necessary components, API routes, and configuration to fetch and render blog posts, with a focus on static generation and SEO best practices. Note that manual setup steps in Basehub are required before the blog will function correctly. ### Highlights * **Basehub CMS Integration**: This PR introduces a new blog feature powered by Basehub CMS, allowing content to be managed directly within the Basehub dashboard. * **Static Generation**: The blog index and post pages are statically generated (`force-static` and `generateStaticParams`), ensuring optimal performance and SEO. * **Rich Text Rendering**: The PR includes a `RichText` renderer with custom element handlers for headings, code blocks, images, and links, providing a flexible content creation experience. * **Environment Variable Validation**: The `BASEHUB_TOKEN` environment variable is validated using T3 Env + Zod, ensuring that the application is properly configured to connect to Basehub. * **Manual Setup Required**: Before this PR can be fully functional, manual steps are required, including creating a Basehub account, defining the Posts collection schema, and setting the `BASEHUB_TOKEN` environment variable. 🧠 **New Feature in Public Preview:** You can now enable **Memory** to help **Gemini Code Assist** learn from your team's feedback.    - Move blog components from `src/components/blog/` to `src/app/(landing)/blog/_components/` (colocated pattern)
   - Check the client-side filtering of `isvisible` in `getAllBlogPosts`

2. **Gemini Code Assist** bot comments:
   - Absolute paths in PRD
   - `params` type as `Promise` (but this is actually correct for Next.js 16+)
   - Missing `BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET` in env/server.ts
   - Client-side filtering of `isvisible`
   - Missing `html`/`markdown` in PostFragment body

3. **Copilot** comments:
   - Client-side filtering of `isvisible` (use server-side filter)
   - Duplicate date formatting logic
   - Duplicate excerpt truncation logic
   - Multiple h1 elements (render h1 from RichText as h2)
   - Code block should use CodeBlock from basehub
   - CoverImage CLS prevention with aspect ratio
   - Error handling in server actions
   - Redundant links in PostCard
   - basehub.config.ts comment
   - Absolute paths in PRD
   - Word-boundary-aware truncation

content.text:
Here's the summary of all comments on PR #19:

---

### Aayush Dutt (@aayushdutt) — **APPROVED** the PR, with 2 comments:

1. **`src/components/blog/avatar.tsx`** — Move blog components to `src/app/(landing)/blog/_components/` instead. Components should be colocated with their route, not in shared `src/components/`, unless used in multiple places.

2. **`src/lib/basehub.action.ts:17`** — "check this" — referring to the client-side `isvisible` filtering in `getAllBlogPosts`.

---

### Gemini Code Assist + GitHub Copilot (bots) — key themes:

| Issue | Files | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| **Move components to colocated `_components/`** | `src/components/blog/*` | Matches Aayush's request |
| **Server-side filter `isvisible`** instead of client-side | `basehub.action.ts` | Both bots flagged |
| **Add `BASEHUB_DRAFT_SECRET` optional env** | `src/env/server.ts` | PRD says to, we skipped it |
| **Rich text h1 → h2** (page already has h1) | `post-body.tsx` | Accessibility |
| **Extract duplicate logic** (date formatting, excerpt truncation) | `post-card.tsx`, `[slug]/page.tsx`, `post-header.tsx` | DRY |
| **Add error handling** (try/catch) to server actions | `basehub.action.ts` | Resilience |
| **Absolute paths in PRD** → use relative paths | `prd-basehub-blog.md` | Portability |
| **CoverImage aspect ratio** to prevent CLS | `cover-image.tsx` | Performance |
| **Make entire PostCard a single link** | `post-card.tsx` | UX/a11y |
| **Word-boundary truncation** for excerpts | `post-card.tsx` | Polish |

---

Want me to address all of these? Aayush's comments are the most important — the component relocation to `_components/` and the server-side `isvisible` filtering.

file.content:
# Project Instructions: runagents

## Tech Stack
- **Framework**: Next.js (App Router)
- **Package Manager**: `pnpm`
- **Environment**: T3 Env + Zod
- **Database**: Neon + Drizzle ORM
- **Auth**: Better Auth + Better Auth UI
- **Styling**: Tailwind CSS + Shadcn UI
- **State**: Zustand (UI only), React Query (server data)
- **Lint/Format**: Biome

## Environment
- Secrets in `.env` (gitignored), template in `.env.example`.

## File Naming
- **Files**: `kebab-case.tsx` — `agent-card.tsx`, `agent.actions.ts`, `agent.repo.ts`
- **Exports**: `PascalCase` — `export function AgentCard() {}`

## Directory Layout

```
src/
├── app/
│   ├── agents/
│   │   ├── page.tsx
│   │   ├── [id]/page.tsx
│   │   ├── _components/               # Colocated, private (not a route)
│   │   ├── _actions/                  # Server actions for this route
│   │   └── _hooks/                    # React Query hooks for this route
│   ├── sign-in/page.tsx
│   ├── sign-up/page.tsx
│   ├── api/auth/[...better-auth]/route.ts
│   ├── layout.tsx
│   ├── page.tsx
│   └── globals.css
├── components/
│   ├── ui/                            # Shadcn primitives (CLI-managed, don't edit)
│   └── *.tsx                          # Shared composed components (flat)
├── hooks/                             # Shared custom hooks
├── lib/                               # External clients & utilities
├── server/
│   ├── action-utils.ts                # authedAction builder, getAuthedSession, parseInput
│   ├── actions/                       # Shared server actions
│   └── db/ (models/, repos/, index.ts)
├── state/                             # Zustand stores
└── env/                               # T3 Env validation
```

## Placement Rules

| Used by… | Location |
|---|---|
| 1 page | `_components/`, `_actions/`, `_hooks/` (colocated) |
| 2+ pages | `components/`, `server/actions/`, `hooks/` (promoted) |
| Shadcn primitive | `components/ui/` (CLI-managed) |

Prefix colocated files with context — `agent-card.tsx` not `card.tsx`.
Keep `components/` flat until 15+ files.
`_components/` only exists inside specific page route folders (e.g. `app/agents/_components/`), never at `app/` root.

## Server Patterns

```
Component → React Query hook → Server Action → Repo → DB
```

| Layer | Location | Does what |
|---|---|---|
| **Repo** | `server/db/repos/` | Pure DB queries. No auth, no validation. |
| **Action** | `_actions/` | Auth + validation + calls repo. |
| **React Query** | `_hooks/` | Client cache, refetching, optimistic UI. Wraps actions. |

### `authedAction` builder (`server/action-utils.ts`)

```ts
// Read — auth only
export const getAgents = authedAction(
  async ({ user }) => agentRepo.findAll(user.id),
);

// Write — auth + Zod validation
export const createAgent = authedAction(createAgentSchema,
  async ({ user }, input) => agentRepo.create({ ...input, userId: user.id }),
);
```

Also exports `getAuthedSession()` and `parseInput()` for direct use.

### When to use what

| Scenario | Pattern |
|---|---|
| Page load, no interactivity | Server Component → repo directly |
| Client needs cache/refetch | React Query → server action → repo |
| Simple form submit | `useTransition` + server action → repo |
| External consumer / webhook | API route → repo |

## Rules

1. Only repos import `db`. Actions never query DB directly.
2. Only actions check auth and validate input. Repos are auth-unaware.
3. Server Components can call repos directly, but must check auth via `getAuthedSession()` first.
4. Promote to shared when used by 2+ pages.
5. Default to Server Components. Add `"use client"` only for interactivity. Action files must start with `"use server"`.
6. `@/` maps to `src/`. Always use `@/` imports, not relative paths.
7. Use `pnpm` exclusively.
8. Use variables via `src/env/`, never `process.env` directly.
9. Use `authClient` (client) and `auth` (server) from `@/lib`.
10. Use `npx shadcn@latest add [component]` to install UI components.
11. Keep models modular in `server/db/models/`, types in `server/db/schema.ts`.
12. Use `pnpm db:generate` for migrations, `pnpm db:push` for dev sync. Ask user before running `db:push`.
13. Use `pnpm auth:generate` after modifying auth config.
14. Run `pnpm lint`, `pnpm format`, `pnpm type-check` to verify changes.

Done. Pushed to PR #19. Here's what changed:

**1. Components colocated** (per Aayush + README rules):
- `src/components/blog/*` → `src/app/(landing)/blog/_components/`
- Renamed with `blog-` prefix: `blog-avatar.tsx`, `blog-cover-image.tsx`, `blog-post-card.tsx`, `blog-post-header.tsx`, `blog-post-body.tsx`

**2. Server-side `isvisible` filtering** (per Aayush):
- `getAllBlogPosts` — added `__args: { filter: { isvisible: { eq: true } }, orderBy: "date__DESC" }` instead of client-side `.filter()` + `.sort()`
- `getAllBlogSlugs` — added `__args: { filter: { isvisible: { eq: true } } }` instead of client-side `.filter()`

message.content: what changes aayush told to change do you please explain ?

content.text:
Aayush left 2 comments on the PR:

### 1. Move blog components to `_components/` (colocated pattern)

**His comment** on `src/components/blog/avatar.tsx`:
> "move the blog components to landing/blog/_components. See README.md for the file structure pattern. components are colocated, unless shared between multiple places."

**What this means:** The project has a rule in README.md — if a component is only used by one route, it lives inside that route's `_components/` folder, not in the shared `src/components/` directory. Our blog components (avatar, cover-image, post-card, post-body, post-header) are only used by the `/blog` pages, so they belong in `src/app/(landing)/blog/_components/`, not `src/components/blog/`.

The `src/components/` folder is reserved for components shared across 2+ routes (like the Logo component used everywhere).

### 2. Server-side `isvisible` filtering

**His comment** on `src/lib/basehub.action.ts:17`:
> "check this"

**What this means:** Our `getAllBlogPosts` was fetching ALL posts from Basehub (including hidden ones), then filtering them out in JavaScript:

```ts
// Before — fetches everything, filters client-side
return blog.posts.items
  .filter((post) => post.isvisible)  // wasteful
  .sort(...)
```

Aayush flagged this because we should tell Basehub to only return visible posts in the query itself:

```ts
// After — Basehub only sends visible posts
__args: {
  filter: { isvisible: { eq: true } },
  orderBy: "date__DESC",
},
```

This is more efficient — less data transferred, less processing, and hidden posts never even reach our code.

Yes, both are implemented and pushed. The commit `7546a72` on PR #19 covers both:

1. **Components moved** — all 5 files relocated from `src/components/blog/` to `src/app/(landing)/blog/_components/` with `blog-` prefix
2. **Server-side filtering** — `getAllBlogPosts` and `getAllBlogSlugs` now use `__args: { filter: { isvisible: { eq: true } } }` instead of client-side `.filter()`

You're welcome! The PR is ready at https://github.com/FounderFeedAdmin/runagents/pull/19 whenever you're ready to merge. Just remember the manual steps (Basehub account, schema setup, `BASEHUB_TOKEN` env var) before it'll work end-to-end.

content.text: The docs show `eq` works with string/slug fields. The error says `isvisible` expects a `string`, not `boolean` — so it's stored as a string `"true"/"false"` in Basehub, not a boolean. This matches what Teammate 1 found from ShowCart (`isvisible` is a string field).
The `isvisible` field is a string type in Basehub (not boolean). Need to change `true` → `"true"`:
